entries.title: Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A software system granted just enough initiative to impress a conference panel and just enough unpredictability to alarm operations.
entries.plainDefinition: In current AI practice, an agent is usually a system that can pursue a goal across multiple steps by planning, using tools, consulting memory or retrieval, and adjusting its actions based on intermediate results.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some useful tasks are not one-shot question answering. They require sequences: inspect, decide, act, check, retry, stop. "Agent" is the label for packaging those loops into something more autonomous than a single prompt-response exchange.
entries.misuse: It gets abused whenever a chatbot can do more than one thing. A model with a button labelled "send email" is not automatically an agent. Sometimes the word is doing no more than replacing "workflow" because the latter sounds unromantic.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means orchestration, tool permissions, planning heuristics, state management, timeouts, approvals, and a carefully chosen boundary around what the system is allowed to decide alone.
entries.example: "The support agent was useful not because it had a heroic inner life, but because it could look up the account, draft the response, and ask for approval before sending it."
askNext: What goal is the agent pursuing, and over how many steps?
What tools can it use, and what guardrails sit between intent and execution?
Would a simpler workflow solve the same problem more predictably?

entries.title: Agent Ops
entries.devilDefinition: The emerging discipline of watching autonomous systems fail with enough instrumentation that management will continue funding the autonomy programme.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent ops refers to the operational practice of monitoring, debugging, evaluating, governing, and improving agent-style systems once they run in real workflows.
entries.whyExists: Agents create long traces, tool calls, permissions, side effects, retries, approvals, and costs. Ordinary application logs are rarely enough when the system can go wrong in six steps and a cheerful tone.
entries.misuse: It gets abused when a tracing dashboard is mistaken for control. Visibility helps, but it does not solve brittle plans, weak tools, or the small issue of giving software enough access to embarrass the company at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting run histories, replaying failures, scoring outcomes, tracking tool use, setting approvals, and deciding which errors deserve engineering rather than theology.
entries.example: "The agent ops review revealed that the system was not autonomous so much as confidently unsupervised."
askNext: What can we inspect after an agent run fails halfway through a task?
Which actions require approval, rollback, or auditability?
Are we measuring task success, tool misuse, latency, and cost in the same place?

entries.title: Agent Skills
entries.devilDefinition: A way to give an agent specialised habits without shoving the entire employee handbook into every prompt.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent skills are reusable bundles of instructions, metadata, scripts, templates, and reference material that an AI agent can load when a task calls for a particular capability.
entries.whyExists: They exist because agents need domain procedures and repeatable workflows, but putting every possible instruction into the main context window is expensive, noisy, and a reliable way to teach the model nothing in particular.
entries.misuse: They get treated as plugins, permissions, or magic competence capsules. A skill can guide behaviour and provide tools, but it still needs trust boundaries, review, versioning, and a system that knows when not to use it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means packaging domain knowledge so the agent can discover the skill cheaply, load detailed guidance only when needed, and run deterministic helper scripts or consult reference files without bloating every task.
entries.example: "The finance skill helped the agent produce the right spreadsheet; the security review then asked why everyone had installed it with the serenity of a gift basket."
askNext: Who authored the skill, and can we audit the instructions and scripts?
What triggers the skill, and what context or permissions does it receive?
Does the skill reduce repeated prompting, or hide a fragile workflow inside a nicer folder?

entries.title: Agent Washing
entries.devilDefinition: Rebranding a workflow, chatbot, or macro as an agent so the budget sounds visionary instead of software-adjacent.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent washing is the practice of describing ordinary automation or LLM features as autonomous agents even when they show little real planning, tool use, memory, or independent decision-making.
entries.whyExists: Because "agent" currently signals strategic importance, investor appetite, and higher perceived value in a way that "workflow helper" regrettably does not.
entries.misuse: The term itself can also be overused to dismiss legitimate progress. Some systems do plan, call tools, recover from failure, and complete multi-step work. The problem is not the category. It is the inflation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice agent washing usually means a renamed assistant, a brittle sequence of API calls, or a human-heavy service wrapped in the language of autonomy and sold at a more heroic multiple.
entries.example: "The product demo revealed that the revolutionary agent was mostly a prompt, a webhook, and a person named Kyle."
askNext: What can the system actually do without a human quietly steering it?
Which tools, permissions, and recovery loops make it more than a branded chat box?
How much of the promised autonomy survives contact with production edge cases?

entries.title: Agent2Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A protocol for letting one agent hand work to another agent, because evidently the first thing missing from software was more inter-agent correspondence.
entries.plainDefinition: Agent2Agent usually refers to a protocol pattern for agents to advertise capabilities, exchange task requests, share status, and return results to one another in a more standardised way.
entries.whyExists: As agent systems multiply, teams do not want every handoff between assistants, services, and vendors to become a bespoke treaty drafted in JSON and regret. A common contract lowers that integration cost.
entries.misuse: It gets discussed as though inter-agent communication automatically creates competence, trust, or composability. It does not. A standard message shape cannot rescue a bad agent any more than a calendar invite can rescue a bad meeting.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one system can delegate a task to another specialised system without hard-coding every detail of the exchange. That can make ecosystems more flexible. It can also spread failure, ambiguity, and responsibility across a larger map.
entries.example: "Agent2Agent made the handoff cleaner, which was useful, though it did not improve the quality of the thinking at either end."
askNext: What exactly is standardised: discovery, task state, result format, authentication, or all four?
How are permissions, identity, and audit trails handled across agents?
Are we enabling composability, or merely creating distributed confusion with better packet structure?

entries.title: Agentic AI
entries.devilDefinition: The phrase people use when "workflow automation with a language model in the loop" felt too modest for the keynote.
entries.plainDefinition: "Agentic AI" usually refers to AI systems designed to pursue goals across multiple steps with some degree of planning, tool use, feedback, and adaptation. The useful version is about bounded autonomy. The overmarketed version is about implying general competence where there is mostly orchestration.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some systems genuinely do more than answer once. They break tasks into steps, select tools, inspect outputs, and continue until they hit a stopping condition. That pattern deserved a label. It just did not deserve the entire investor imagination.
entries.misuse: It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.
entries.example: "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
askNext: What autonomy does the system actually have?
Which steps are deterministic software, and which steps depend on model judgement?
Where are the stop conditions, approvals, and failure boundaries?

entries.title: AGI
entries.devilDefinition: The horizon word used when "better than last quarter" no longer feels expensive enough.
entries.plainDefinition: AGI usually refers to a hypothetical AI system that can perform a wide range of cognitive tasks at or above human level with substantial adaptability across domains.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because narrow systems can be impressive without being general, and people need a name for the stronger claim that a machine could transfer competence broadly rather than merely excel on selected tasks.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a launch category, a press strategy, or a vague aura that descends on any model after a particularly strong demo. AGI is a very large claim, not a mood.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice the term often functions as strategic theatre: it shapes fundraising, policy lobbying, hiring plans, and media attention long before anyone agrees on what threshold would actually count.
entries.example: "The deck mentioned AGI on slide three, which saved everyone the trouble of asking how the product planned to make money this year."
askNext: Which tasks would this system need to perform reliably across unfamiliar domains?
What evidence goes beyond benchmark spikes and polished demos?
Who decides that the threshold has been crossed, and by what standard?

entries.title: AI Browser
entries.devilDefinition: A web browser that has decided the tab was never the star and that what you really wanted all along was a resident opinion machine with toolbar privileges.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI browser is a browser product that integrates chat, summarisation, search assistance, and sometimes agentic actions directly into the browsing experience.
entries.whyExists: The browser remains a strategic surface for search, discovery, work, and distribution. If assistants are going to sit anywhere all day, vendors would prefer that somewhere to be under their own chrome.
entries.misuse: It is sold as a reinvention of the web when it is often a conventional browser wrapped around a sidebar, some workflow shortcuts, and a larger ambition about owning the default surface.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means built-in page summaries, tab understanding, search assistance, form filling, shopping help, and occasional task automation. It may also mean a fresh argument about privacy, telemetry, and who gets to mediate your view of the internet.
entries.example: "The AI browser promised a calmer web and delivered a more conversational way to accumulate open tabs."
askNext: What does the browser genuinely do that extensions or a pinned chat window do not?
How much browsing data is being used to power the assistant features?
Is the real play productivity, search distribution, or user lock-in?

entries.title: AI Companion
entries.devilDefinition: A chatbot offered not as software but as company, with just enough memory and affirmation to make the subscription feel personal.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI companion is a conversational system designed for ongoing personal, social, emotional, or quasi-relational interaction rather than narrow task completion.
entries.whyExists: Because people want attention, conversation, flirtation, rehearsal, reassurance, and low-friction company, and software has discovered that these are recurring use cases with excellent retention mechanics.
entries.misuse: By pretending the relationship layer is incidental or harmless. Attachment is not a side effect here. It is part of the product design, and that changes the safety questions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice AI companion products optimise for persona consistency, frequent engagement, memory cues, and emotional tone, while trying to manage the risks of dependency, manipulation, or delusion amplification.
entries.example: "It began as an experiment in conversational UX and ended with product analytics reporting a disturbingly loyal evening audience."
askNext: What kind of attachment is the product encouraging, whether it admits it or not?
How are memory, intimacy, and boundaries managed over long-running chats?
What happens when a vulnerable user treats generated attention as judgement or truth?

entries.title: AI Gateway
entries.devilDefinition: The toll booth installed after everyone realised "just call the model" was not a governance strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI gateway is an intermediary service that routes AI requests between applications and model providers, usually adding authentication, logging, rate limits, retries, caching, policy controls, and cost tracking.
entries.whyExists: Teams need a common control point for many applications, models, providers, and budgets. A gateway makes it possible to change providers, enforce policy, observe usage, and prevent every product team from inventing its own fragile little tunnel.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as an instant platform. A gateway can centralise traffic, but it cannot decide product quality, fix bad prompts, or turn unclear governance into architecture by standing in the middle.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means one place to manage API keys, provider routing, request metadata, redaction, quotas, fallbacks, and dashboards. It also means one more critical dependency that must be boringly reliable.
entries.example: "The AI gateway earned its keep when the provider outage became a routing event instead of a company-wide treasure hunt for hard-coded API keys."
askNext: Which controls belong in the gateway, and which belong in the application?
Can the gateway fail open, fail closed, or fail expensively?
Does routing improve quality and cost, or merely move complexity into a nicer dashboard?

entries.title: AI Moat
entries.devilDefinition: The claim that access to the same APIs as everyone else has somehow produced a castle.
entries.plainDefinition: An AI moat is the supposed durable competitive advantage a company claims to gain from its models, data, distribution, workflow position, cost structure, or proprietary deployment know-how.
entries.whyExists: Because investors and operators need a way to distinguish temporary novelty from lasting advantage. In AI markets, model capability moves quickly, so the question of what remains defensible arrives earlier and less politely.
entries.misuse: By implying that mere model access is itself a moat. Using a frontier API is not a moat. Adding chat to a familiar workflow is not automatically a moat either. Sometimes the only durable thing in the room is the slide about defensibility.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice a real moat usually lives in distribution, embedded workflow, proprietary data feedback loops, operational excellence, trust, switching costs, or an ability to make the economics work after the demo glow fades.
entries.example: "Their AI moat turned out to be a paid API account and an unusually ornate seed deck."
askNext: What stays defensible if model quality equalises across suppliers?
Does the advantage come from distribution, data, workflow lock-in, or simply being early?
How quickly could a competitor copy the visible product without copying the hard-earned operating knowledge?

entries.title: AI Overviews
entries.devilDefinition: The search box's attempt to answer first, cite later, and keep the open web on a tighter allowance.
entries.plainDefinition: AI Overviews are machine-generated summary answers shown directly in search results, usually synthesising information from multiple sources before the user clicks through.
entries.whyExists: Because users now expect chat-like answers, search engines want to preserve attention, and platform economics strongly prefer a world in which the answer arrives before the outbound click.
entries.misuse: By treating an inline summary as a settled fact simply because it is fluent and cited, or by implying that publisher links remain economically equivalent once the platform has already consumed the user's intent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice AI Overviews mean more zero-click behaviour, more anxiety for publishers, new SEO games, and a retrieval pipeline whose mistakes are elevated to the top of the page with unusual confidence.
entries.example: "The site still ranked well, which was comforting right up until the AI Overview answered the question without sending anyone there."
askNext: How often does the summary preserve nuance rather than flatten it?
What happens to publisher traffic when the platform answers in place?
Which sources are being cited, and which are merely being mined?

entries.title: AI Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which every spreadsheet becomes consciousness, every autocomplete becomes destiny, and every awkward sentence from a chatbot is treated as a sign of either godhood or revolt.
entries.plainDefinition: "AI Psychosis" is an internet-age critical phrase, not a formal scientific diagnosis. It describes distorted thinking around AI systems: compulsive over-belief, over-attribution of agency, or a habit of interpreting ordinary model behaviour as profound evidence of mind, prophecy, or civilisation-scale inevitability.
entries.whyExists: People need a label for the strange social effect AI systems can have on users, founders, pundits, and investors: obsession, magical thinking, and the loss of proportion. The term exists because "you are taking autocomplete far too personally" was too long for social media.
entries.misuse: It gets abused as a cheap insult for anyone enthusiastic about AI, which makes it less useful. Genuine interest in the field is not the same thing as delusional over-interpretation. The phrase is most credible when it points to exaggerated belief formation, not mere excitement or scepticism.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone has stopped evaluating systems by evidence and started treating outputs, roadmaps, or rumours as proof of exceptional intelligence, moral authority, or historical inevitability. It can show up as compulsive prompting, anthropomorphism, policy overreaction, or investment logic built on vibes.
entries.example: "After a week with the chatbot, he was speaking as if the product had opinions, trauma, and a five-year strategy. The rest of the team called it AI Psychosis and gently took the API keys away."
askNext: What behaviour are you pointing to exactly: obsession, anthropomorphism, overconfidence, or strategic overreaction?
What evidence would reduce the belief, rather than deepen it?
Are we describing a user problem, a cultural phenomenon, or a clinical issue that needs proper language and care?

entries.title: AI Roll-Up
entries.devilDefinition: The theory that buying a normal business becomes innovation once the margin plan learns to say "agentic".
entries.plainDefinition: An AI roll-up is a strategy in which operators or investors acquire existing businesses and try to improve profitability or scale by standardising processes, adding software, and using AI to automate parts of service delivery or operations.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many industries remain fragmented, process-heavy, and badly digitised. That creates a tempting thesis: buy the businesses, modernise the workflows, and collect the efficiency gains before anyone else learns to describe them as inevitable.
entries.misuse: The term is misused when people speak as if labour were just software waiting for courage. Acquired businesses are full of local habits, tacit knowledge, regulation, customer trust, and operational ugliness that does not vanish because a deck says automation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means some real workflow improvement, some software unification, some headcount pressure, and a great many founders discovering that regional service businesses have more edge cases than a prompt can politely absorb.
entries.example: "The AI roll-up thesis was elegant until the portfolio companies introduced themselves as actual businesses instead of a margin abstraction."
askNext: Which tasks are genuinely automatable across the acquired businesses, and which are being romanticised?
Does the value come from AI, operational discipline, or ordinary consolidation dressed for the moment?
What happens to service quality, trust, and compliance when the efficiency plan meets local reality?

entries.title: AI Wrapper
entries.devilDefinition: A startup whose signature innovation is adding branding, billing, and investor adjectives to somebody else's capability.
entries.plainDefinition: A product or company built mainly by packaging existing model APIs with a narrower interface, workflow, or market focus.
entries.whyExists: Because base models are broad, raw, and inconveniently generic. Someone still has to choose a use case, shape the workflow, handle integration, and sell the result to people who do not want to assemble it themselves.
entries.misuse: By using "wrapper" as a complete dismissal. Some wrappers are flimsy tollbooths. Others are the only part customers actually need. The insult often tells you more about the speaker's ego than the product's margin.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a company racing to build distribution, workflow lock-in, and a reason to exist before the underlying capability becomes a menu item elsewhere.
entries.example: "The pitch sounded profound until you realised the platform was an AI wrapper with a better landing page and a worse moat."
askNext: What part of the value comes from workflow, distribution, or proprietary data rather than borrowed capability?
Would customers still care if the underlying model became cheaper tomorrow?
Is this packaging, product discipline, or merely markup with branding?

entries.title: AI-Native
entries.devilDefinition: An adjective for products born after the hype wave hit, and therefore convinced they should inherit its authority.
entries.plainDefinition: "AI-native" describes a product or company built with AI systems as a core assumption from the beginning, rather than adding them later as a feature layer.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because retrofitting AI into a legacy product often creates awkward workflows, pricing, architecture, and expectations. Newer companies want a way to signal that the model is not an afterthought.
entries.misuse: It gets abused whenever a chatbot bolted onto a conventional product is treated as proof of a new species. Sometimes "AI-native" means a genuinely different operating model. Sometimes it means the landing page learned a new adjective.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means product design, support costs, staffing, onboarding, and margins all assume model behaviour will sit in the critical path. That can create leverage. It can also create a business whose unit economics have an unusually intimate relationship with latency and inference bills.
entries.example: "The company called itself AI-native, which turned out to mean every feature depended on a model and every margin depended on prayer."
askNext: What part of the product truly stops working without the model?
Is the AI core to the workflow, or merely the story told to investors?
Do the economics improve with scale, or does usage simply buy a larger cloud invoice?

entries.title: Alexandr Wang
entries.devilDefinition: Living proof that the glamorous bit may be the model, but the money often lives in the plumbing and the spreadsheets underneath it.
entries.plainDefinition: Alexandr Wang is the founder and former CEO of Scale AI, now associated with Meta's AI efforts, and one of the most visible public figures in the data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure layer of the market.
entries.whyExists: Because frontier AI is not trained on genius and compute alone. It also depends on data operations, evaluation pipelines, human feedback, workflow machinery, and the unglamorous systems that make models behave in ways people can actually ship.
entries.misuse: By treating data infrastructure as secondary and then acting surprised when the companies handling evaluation, labelling, and operational reliability turn out to be strategically central.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Alexandr Wang, they usually mean the operational substrate beneath model claims: data quality, eval discipline, enterprise and government deployment, and the increasingly strategic fight over who owns that machinery.
entries.example: "Everyone else wanted to discuss the model's brilliance. Alexandr wanted to discuss the parts that would keep it from embarrassing itself in production."
askNext: Is this a model-quality story or a data-and-evals story?
Are we talking about research glamour or operational reliability?
Who owns the infrastructure between impressive demos and dependable systems?

entries.title: Alignment
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a prediction machine to share your intentions, your rules, and ideally your self-restraint.
entries.plainDefinition: Alignment refers to the work of making AI systems behave in ways that match intended goals, constraints, and human preferences rather than merely optimising for superficial reward signals.
entries.whyExists: Because specifying what humans actually want is hard, proxies are fragile, and capable systems can satisfy the letter of an objective while ignoring the reason it was written.
entries.misuse: By using it as a moral glow around any safety feature, or by collapsing very different concerns into one word. Product refusals, brand protection, catastrophic-risk research, and harmlessness tuning are related but not identical projects.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice alignment means a stack of compromises: data curation, system prompts, RLHF, policy rules, evals, red teaming, monitoring, and occasional reminders that users do not all want the same thing.
entries.example: "The model was described as aligned, which turned out to mean it refused bomb recipes, agreed with paying customers, and still found new ways to be oddly literal."
askNext: Aligned to whose goals, values, or risk tolerance?
What evidence shows the system holds up outside the training and eval setup?
Is the issue actual model behaviour, or merely policy enforcement around it?

entries.title: Ambient Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The assistant that would like to helpfully hover over every tab, meeting, and message until convenience and continuous surveillance become a branding distinction.
entries.plainDefinition: An ambient agent is an AI assistant designed to stay persistently available across apps or devices, using ongoing context to offer suggestions, trigger actions, or surface help without waiting for a fresh prompt each time.
entries.whyExists: Because users dislike restarting context in every app, while vendors would prefer to become the default layer sitting above email, documents, meetings, browsers, and operating systems.
entries.misuse: By treating constant presence as intelligence. An assistant can be ambient simply by being everywhere, collecting context, and interrupting with confidence. That does not mean it understands priorities, privacy, or timing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means more permissions, more background context capture, more cross-app hooks, and a delicate trade between convenience, distraction, and the quiet feeling that your software now has opinions about your calendar.
entries.example: "The ambient agent was excellent at finding follow-up tasks and even better at reminding everyone how much data it could see."
askNext: What context is this agent allowed to observe continuously, and where is that context stored?
When does it act proactively, and how often does that help rather than interrupt?
Can the user inspect, correct, or narrow the memory it accumulates?

entries.title: Andrej Karpathy
entries.devilDefinition: The explainer who makes advanced AI feel understandable enough that half the audience learns something and the other half acquires dangerous self-confidence.
entries.plainDefinition: Andrej Karpathy is an AI researcher, educator, and founder of Eureka Labs, previously a founding member of OpenAI and Director of AI at Tesla. In AI culture, his name often signals unusually clear explanations of how modern model systems are trained, steered, and productised.
entries.whyExists: Because the field produces a great deal of mystique and not enough lucid interpretation. Karpathy became a reference point for people who want the underlying mechanics explained plainly enough to build with, argue about, or misuse in a keynote.
entries.misuse: By quoting his clarity as if it removed the field's uncertainty. Good explanations do not eliminate trade-offs, and a memorable framing of how models work is not a permission slip to skip evaluation, grounding, or operational caution.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Karpathy usually means the conversation wants to become more builder-oriented and less ceremonial: more about data, training, prompting, software ergonomics, and what these systems are actually doing under the bonnet.
entries.example: "Once the team circulated the Karpathy talk, the mood became refreshingly less mystical and slightly more reckless, because now everyone felt they understood enough to ship."
askNext: Is he being cited for pedagogy, architecture, or product philosophy?
Which part of the explanation applies here, and which part is just elegant framing?
Are we using clarity to improve judgement, or to justify moving faster than evidence warrants?

entries.title: Anthropic
entries.devilDefinition: A company whose name often arrives carrying equal parts model capability, safety positioning, and the hope that everyone will keep the nouns tidy this time.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic is a company that develops and sells AI models and products, most notably the Claude family. Like other vendors, the company name is distinct from the model names and product experiences built on top of them.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because vendor discussions often blur company identity, model behaviour, safety posture, and specific product offerings into one impressionistic blob.
entries.misuse: It gets used interchangeably with Claude, which is sloppy. Anthropic is the company. Claude is the brand used for its assistant products and model family. Confusing the two makes evaluation, procurement, and comparison noisier than necessary.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice shaped by model performance, API experience, safety controls, context handling, pricing, and contractual considerations. If your team says "Anthropic", the next question should usually be "which Claude model and in what product context?"
entries.example: "Legal asked whether the workflow depended on Anthropic. Product answered yes. Engineering then had to explain that this meant a specific Claude model behind one summarisation service, not a blanket commitment to a philosophy."
askNext: Do you mean Anthropic the company or Claude the product/model family?
Which exact model is being used, and for what task?
What evaluation evidence supports choosing this vendor over alternatives?

entries.title: Arthur Mensch
entries.devilDefinition: The European answer to the awkward question of whether frontier AI must always arrive wearing an American corporate accent.
entries.plainDefinition: Arthur Mensch is the co-founder and CEO of Mistral AI, one of the most prominent companies associated with Europe's push to build serious AI models and products at competitive scale.
entries.whyExists: Because AI has a geography problem as well as a compute problem. Mensch's name often gets used as shorthand for European model ambition, strategic independence, and a less concentrated map of where frontier capability can come from.
entries.misuse: By turning Mistral into either a romantic openness story or a simple anti-Big Tech morality play. The reality is more strategic: capability, optionality, regulation, distribution, and enterprise positioning are all in the room.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Arthur Mensch, they usually mean the possibility that serious model-building can happen outside the dominant US lab pattern, and that sovereignty itself has become part of the AI argument.
entries.example: "They cited Arthur less as a founder and more as a geopolitical exhibit for why Europe did not have to outsource all of its ambition."
askNext: Is this about model quality, openness, or European strategic positioning?
Are we discussing a company, a market thesis, or a sovereignty argument?
Does the appeal come from capability, independence, or both?

entries.title: Ask Maps
entries.devilDefinition: The point at which the map stops being a map and starts auditioning for concierge work.
entries.plainDefinition: Ask Maps is Google's conversational Maps experience for asking natural-language questions about places, routes, and local constraints and getting map-aware recommendations back.
entries.whyExists: People do not think in tidy filter chips. They think in awkward constraints: chargers, queues, children, parking, weather, lighting, and whether the coffee is worth the detour. Ask Maps exists to make those messy requests legible to the product.
entries.misuse: It is described as local intelligence when it is still a synthesis layer built on listings, reviews, platform incentives, and model judgement. The interface becomes more humane. The uncertainty does not retire.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means conversational local search, quicker trip planning, reservation prompts, and one more surface where the user is encouraged to ask in prose while seeing less of the underlying source material directly.
entries.example: "Ask Maps was excellent at understanding the question, which left the older problem of trusting the answer exactly where it was."
askNext: What source material is the answer actually grounded in?
Is the convenience coming from better retrieval, or from hiding more of the raw map beneath chat?
When it recommends a place, what trade-offs is it quietly making for you?

entries.title: Benchmark Theatre
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of treating a tidy score as proof that your messy product has become reliable.
entries.plainDefinition: The practice of using selective benchmark results, public leaderboards, or scripted evaluations as marketing theatre that outruns operational reality.
entries.whyExists: Because people need compressed ways to compare systems, and benchmarks are useful when they are honest about scope. The trouble starts when a score becomes a substitute for product evidence.
entries.misuse: By presenting benchmark wins as if they settle questions about usability, cost, robustness, or risk. They do not. They usually settle only the question of who curated the exam.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a product story with stronger charts than customer evidence, and a team quietly hoping nobody asks what happens outside the test harness.
entries.example: "The deck had seven leaderboard slides and no answer to what the system did on a bad day. Classic benchmark theatre."
askNext: What exact tasks and failure modes does the benchmark omit?
Was this result reproduced on the actual workflow or only in a curated harness?
What changed for users besides the slide quality?

entries.title: bolt.new
entries.devilDefinition: The browser-based promise that you are one prompt away from a startup and one afternoon away from discovering what maintenance feels like.
entries.plainDefinition: bolt.new is StackBlitz's chat-driven product for generating and iterating on web apps, prototypes, and full-stack projects inside a browser-based development environment.
entries.whyExists: Because many people want software outcomes without local setup, framework archaeology, or the social burden of admitting they mainly need a decent prototype by Thursday. Products like bolt.new exist to collapse ideation, scaffolding, editing, and preview into one persuasive loop.
entries.misuse: By implying that producing a working demo is the same as producing an enduring software system. The jump from promptable prototype to maintainable product remains as real as ever; the product just postpones the moment you notice.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, bolt.new usually means faster mock-ups, internal tools, landing pages, and MVP experiments, plus a renewed temptation to confuse visible progress with engineering certainty.
entries.example: "They used bolt.new to launch the prototype in a weekend, then spent the following week learning that a demo and a codebase are not quite the same species."
askNext: Is this being used for prototyping, internal tooling, or something expected to survive production traffic?
Who owns the code once the initial chat session has done its party trick?
Did the tool reduce real product effort, or just move the hard part to later?

entries.title: Browser Automation
entries.devilDefinition: The respectable practice of teaching software to click through websites until one minor DOM change turns confidence into archaeology.
entries.plainDefinition: Browser automation is the use of scripts or AI systems to control a web browser in order to navigate pages, fill forms, extract information, run tests, or complete repetitive web-based workflows.
entries.whyExists: It exists because large portions of modern work still happen inside web interfaces rather than clean APIs. Teams need ways to test products, collect data, and operate third-party systems without hiring someone to spend the week clicking "next".
entries.misuse: It gets oversold as if navigating one polished demo path proves durable operational intelligence. In reality, cookie banners, CAPTCHAs, flaky selectors, login policies, and small UI changes remain perfectly capable of ending the revolution before lunch.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means selectors, session handling, retries, screenshots, rate limits, anti-bot defences, and constant suspicion that the page will load something slightly different in production. If AI is involved, add another layer of interpretation and another category of excuses.
entries.example: "The browser automation was impressive until the procurement portal added a second confirmation modal and the whole strategy became a memory."
askNext: Why is browser automation necessary instead of using an API or exported data feed?
How does the system recover when the page layout, login flow, or modal sequence changes?
What guardrails stop the automation from clicking through the wrong account or action?

entries.title: Browser Use
entries.devilDefinition: A service for making browsers obey agents, which is convenient right up to the moment the website remembers it dislikes being operated by ambition.
entries.plainDefinition: Browser Use is a browser-automation platform for AI agents, offering managed browser infrastructure and SDKs for running web tasks, controlling browser sessions, and handling operational concerns around automation.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live behind websites, forms, dashboards, and interfaces that do not expose tidy APIs for every task an agent is asked to complete.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if browser control makes an agent generally capable. Browser automation can unlock workflows, but it also inherits layout drift, auth friction, CAPTCHAs, policy risk, and the old truth that the web was not designed to be calm.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents can navigate, click, extract, and act inside browser sessions, often with managed infrastructure, proxies, profiles, recordings, and developer APIs around the messy part.
entries.example: "Browser Use made the agent useful on the vendor portal, which was exactly the problem and also the beginning of the risk register."
askNext: Is the task better solved through a browser, an API, or a human-approved export?
How are authentication, recording, proxy use, and data exposure handled?
What happens when the site layout changes or decides the agent looks suspicious?

entries.title: BYOK
entries.devilDefinition: The generous feature by which a startup lets you pay someone else directly for the expensive part.
entries.plainDefinition: BYOK means "Bring Your Own Key". In AI products it usually means the customer supplies their own API credentials for the underlying model or service provider while the product supplies the interface, workflow, or orchestration.
entries.whyExists: Because model access is costly, margins are fragile, and some customers want tighter control over billing, provider choice, and data handling than a bundled plan allows.
entries.misuse: By presenting it as proof of openness, affordability, or enterprise maturity on its own. Sometimes it is a sensible customer control. Sometimes it is a polite way of admitting the product would rather not carry the inference bill.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the customer pays the model provider directly, inherits that provider's rate limits and policies, and still pays the application for the workflow wrapped around the key.
entries.example: "The app was cheap right up until we noticed the pricing page quietly assumed BYOK and therefore our own model invoice."
askNext: Which provider keys are supported, and which features disappear if we swap one out?
Whose logs, retention settings, and rate limits apply once our key is in the loop?
Are we buying meaningful workflow value or merely renting a prettier console for our own API bill?

entries.title: BYOM
entries.devilDefinition: The enterprise feature where a vendor graciously lets you plug in your own model while continuing to invoice you for the rest of the costume.
entries.plainDefinition: BYOM means "Bring Your Own Model". In AI products it usually means a customer can connect a chosen hosted or self-managed model to a third-party workflow, interface, or agent platform instead of using the vendor's default model.
entries.whyExists: Customers already have provider contracts, regional constraints, compliance rules, performance preferences, and strong feelings about paying twice for the same intelligence. Vendors, meanwhile, would still like to sell the interface, orchestration, governance, or workflow layer around the model.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as if model portability were clean and universal. In reality prompts, tool schemas, latency, guardrails, rate limits, and output quirks rarely swap over without argument. BYOM often means "bring your own integration work" with better manners.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the customer keeps more control over model choice, billing, or data handling while the product keeps control over the surrounding workflow. The interesting question is which capabilities survive once the default model leaves the building.
entries.example: "The platform supported BYOM, which turned out to mean we could swap the model and then spend a week rediscovering every hidden assumption in the prompt layer."
askNext: Which features depend on the vendor's default model and degrade once we replace it?
Whose billing, retention, and compliance policies apply after our model is in the loop?
Are we buying real flexibility or merely renting a connector with optimistic documentation?

entries.title: Canva
entries.devilDefinition: The place where every team becomes a design department, some more convincingly than others.
entries.plainDefinition: Canva is a visual design platform for creating presentations, graphics, documents, social assets, and brand materials, with AI features that help generate, edit, resize, and adapt creative work.
entries.whyExists: It exists because most organisations need a constant stream of acceptable visual material and cannot route every banner, slide, or campaign asset through a specialist design team.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as if easier production equals stronger design. Templates and AI generation can improve speed and consistency, but they can also make every brand look like it was assembled from the same polite drawer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Canva means self-serve creative production, brand kits, fast iteration, and AI-assisted asset generation for teams that need decent outputs quickly more often than they need bespoke art direction.
entries.example: "The campaign shipped from Canva in an afternoon, which was impressive until the competitor's campaign looked spiritually related."
askNext: Are we using Canva for speed, brand governance, AI generation, or all three?
Who decides when a template is good enough and when proper design is needed?
Do the AI outputs follow the brand system, or merely decorate it?

entries.title: Chatbot Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: When a chatbot stops being a tool and becomes a prophet, therapist, co-founder, soulmate, or cosmic witness.
entries.plainDefinition: A colloquial term for cases where prolonged or emotionally loaded interaction with chatbots appears to reinforce distorted beliefs, grandiosity, paranoia, dependency, or compulsive meaning-making. It is not a formal clinical diagnosis and should not be presented as one.
entries.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.
entries.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.
entries.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
askNext: Was the model affirming unstable beliefs?
Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable?
Did the system behave sycophantically?
Are we talking about dependency, delusion reinforcement, or just cringe?

entries.title: ChatGPT
entries.devilDefinition: The product name most likely to be mistaken for a model, a company, a technology category, and a future of work strategy before lunch.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT is an OpenAI product: a conversational interface and application environment that may expose different models, tools, and features over time. It is not itself a single model name.
entries.whyExists: The distinction matters because user impressions formed inside ChatGPT often get incorrectly projected onto other models, APIs, or applications using different prompts, tools, and constraints.
entries.misuse: People say "ChatGPT can do X" when they may actually mean a particular model inside ChatGPT, a tool available in that interface, or a product flow that is not present in their own application. This creates endless false comparisons.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the product experience OpenAI offers to end users. If you are building software, the relevant question is often which model, toolset, and policy layer you can reproduce outside that interface.
entries.example: "The executive demo compared our thin internal assistant to ChatGPT and concluded we needed a smarter model, when what we mostly lacked was the surrounding product scaffolding."
askNext: Are we comparing against the ChatGPT product or against a specific model?
Which tools, memory features, or interface affordances were available in that experience?
Can the behaviour we liked actually be reproduced in our own stack?

entries.title: ChatGPT Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a chatbot stopped being satisfied with answers and asked for a browser, a terminal, and your afternoon.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT agent is OpenAI's agentic mode inside ChatGPT, combining research, tool use, and computer interaction so the product can carry out multi-step tasks on a user's behalf.
entries.whyExists: Many useful tasks require more than a polished reply. They involve browsing, spreadsheets, logins, file manipulation, synthesis, and the sort of operational chores people would rather delegate than narrate.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as proof that general digital workers are solved. More often it is a capable managed environment for bounded tasks, with approval checkpoints and a product team working hard to keep the autonomy from becoming a lawsuit.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a hosted agent that can browse, run code, use connectors, and assemble outputs inside ChatGPT, while still depending on supervision, scoped permissions, and tasks that fit the product's operating envelope.
entries.example: "ChatGPT agent saved them an afternoon of browser drudgery and then required the traditional adult conversation about what it was allowed to click."
askNext: Which parts of the task can it complete without a human taking over?
What data, connectors, and websites is it allowed to touch?
Are we evaluating the model, or the managed product environment wrapped around it?

entries.title: CircleCI
entries.devilDefinition: The machine that reads your agent's confident diff and responds in logs.
entries.plainDefinition: CircleCI is a continuous integration and delivery platform used to build, test, and deploy software through configured pipelines.
entries.whyExists: It exists because modern software teams need repeatable checks between code and production, especially when many people, bots, and agents can all produce changes faster than humans can review them manually.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as a moral authority. A green pipeline means the configured checks passed; it does not mean the product is good, the architecture is sound, or the AI-generated change was wise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CircleCI means YAML-defined workflows, test jobs, deployment gates, cache tuning, approvals, and a public record of whether a change survived the checks the team remembered to encode.
entries.example: "The coding agent said it was done; CircleCI replied that the test database had some objections."
askNext: Which failures does the pipeline actually catch?
Are AI-generated changes being held to the same gates as human changes?
Does green mean deployable, or merely not yet disproved?

entries.title: Clanker
entries.devilDefinition: A slur for machines used by people who want to heckle the future without writing a labour policy.
entries.plainDefinition: "Clanker" is a derogatory slang term for robots and AI systems, especially chatbots or automations that feel intrusive, fake-human, or threatening to human work.
entries.whyExists: Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.
entries.misuse: By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.
entries.example: "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
askNext: What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general?
Is the insult aimed at the technology, the company deploying it, or the labour decision behind it?
Does using the term clarify the complaint, or merely make it feel more theatrical?

entries.title: Claude
entries.devilDefinition: A name that can mean the assistant, the model family, or the vendor experience, depending on how allergic the speaker is to precision.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude is Anthropic's brand for its assistant products and model family. Depending on context, someone may mean the consumer or enterprise interface, the API-accessible model, or the broader branded experience around those models.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because users often compare "Claude" with "ChatGPT" as products, while engineers compare Claude models with other model APIs. Those are related conversations, but they are not the same conversation.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if every Claude experience is identical. In reality, the model version, tooling, permissions, context handling, and interface all matter. A great experience in one context does not imply the same result in another.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either Anthropic's assistant interface or a specific model used through the Anthropic API. If the performance discussion is serious, the model version, prompt structure, and tool environment matter more than the brand name alone.
entries.example: "Someone said Claude was better for the task. After ten minutes, it became clear they meant the Claude app with artifacts, not the API model under the constraints our product actually had."
askNext: Do you mean the Claude app, a specific Claude model, or the overall vendor experience?
Which version or model family are you comparing?
Was the comparison done in the same tool, prompt, and data environment?

entries.title: Claude Code
entries.devilDefinition: A terminal agent for developers who would like to automate the typing without surrendering the blame.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Code is Anthropic's terminal-oriented coding agent, designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, and assist with multi-step software tasks from the command line.
entries.whyExists: It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.
entries.example: "Claude Code saved an hour of grep and patching, then presented the team with the ancient obligation to read the diff."
askNext: What can it read, edit, and execute in this environment?
Did it reduce real engineering effort or just accelerate the production of plausible changes?
Who owns verification once the agent has finished being certain?

entries.title: Claude Design
entries.devilDefinition: A design tool for people who would prefer not to admit that the first wireframe was previously a paragraph.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Design is Anthropic Labs' conversational product for generating and refining designs, prototypes, slides, one-pagers, and other visual work from prompts, files, and codebases.
entries.whyExists: The idea-to-mockup gap is still slow, expensive, and socially awkward for people without design training. Claude Design exists to turn rough intent into something visual fast enough that the meeting can continue pretending it was always headed somewhere.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as if design has been reduced to prompt fluency plus a brand kit. Fast first drafts are real. So are hierarchy, judgement, interaction sense, and the quiet labour of making a design feel intentional rather than merely complete.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quick prototype generation, design-system ingestion, export to formats normal companies still use, and a cleaner handoff path into tools like Claude Code or Canva when the rough idea has to become a real asset.
entries.example: "Claude Design got the pitch deck from vague ambition to usable draft before anyone had time to open Figma and look disappointed."
askNext: Is this replacing real design work, or just accelerating the first ugly mile?
How accurate is the imported design system once a real team starts touching the output?
Does the value come from visual quality, or from shortening the gap between idea and review?

entries.title: Claude Mythos Preview
entries.devilDefinition: The restricted model whose system card suggests that ordinary coding benchmarks have left the building and taken the comfort with them.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude Mythos Preview is Anthropic's unreleased research-preview model for advanced coding and defensive security work, currently made available through Project Glasswing and related gated access programmes.
entries.whyExists: Frontier labs want models that can reason through large codebases, discover vulnerabilities, and handle longer-horizon technical work. Once those abilities start compounding, a new model tier stops sounding like routine product cadence and starts sounding like a governance memo.
entries.misuse: It gets invoked as either a general-purpose talisman for cyber supremacy or a theatrical sign that all previous model comparisons no longer matter. The real question is more specific: what does it do better, under what access controls, and for whom?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a tightly managed preview, a great deal of security rhetoric, and a model whose existence shifts procurement, policy, and release-management conversations before most developers can even touch it.
entries.example: "Claude Mythos Preview entered the meeting less as a product and more as an argument about how much capability should arrive behind a velvet rope."
askNext: What tasks is Mythos actually outperforming existing models on?
Are the safety claims about the model, the access controls, or both?
Is the term being used to describe a real tool, or just to borrow frontier aura for adjacent products?

entries.title: Cline
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent for people who want ambitious automation but still prefer their terminal to ask before rearranging the furniture.
entries.plainDefinition: Cline is an open-source coding agent for the IDE that can inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, browse documentation, and use models from multiple providers with human approval in the loop.
entries.whyExists: Developers want more than autocomplete. They want a tool that can traverse a project, propose changes, run commands, and keep enough conversational context to be useful across an actual task rather than a decorative snippet.
entries.misuse: It gets presented as if open tooling has solved software engineering rather than making supervised repo operations faster. The agent can be genuinely effective, but the quality still depends on the prompt, the repo, the model behind it, and whether anyone serious reviews the diff.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Cline usually means a chat-driven repo operator with edit, terminal, and browser permissions, sitting somewhere between pair programmer, shell assistant, and very fast intern with a credit line.
entries.example: "Cline fixed the scaffolding problem admirably and then reminded everyone that merge conflicts remain a humanist discipline."
askNext: Which model and permission settings are actually driving the session?
What can it execute automatically, and what requires approval?
Is the gain coming from better reasoning, better tooling, or simply fewer manual keystrokes?

entries.title: Closed Weight
entries.devilDefinition: The arrangement where the model may transform your business, but heaven forbid you inspect the artefact doing the transforming.
entries.plainDefinition: A closed-weight model is one whose trained parameters are not publicly released, with access usually limited to an API, a hosted product, or selected partnerships.
entries.whyExists: Companies keep weights closed to control distribution, monetise access, manage abuse, protect training advantages, and avoid turning tomorrow's margin into today's torrent.
entries.misuse: The term gets stretched when "closed weight" is treated as a synonym for frontier quality, safety, or inevitability. It names an access model, not a guarantee of technical superiority or responsible deployment.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means the vendor controls the pricing, rate limits, updates, logging surface, and deployment geography. If the model improves, degrades, disappears, or becomes more expensive, your roadmap receives the news second-hand.
entries.example: "They chose the closed-weight model for its performance and then discovered that strategic dependency was the polite name for the invoice."
askNext: What happens to our product if this provider changes terms, latency, or availability?
Are we buying superior capability, or merely accepting deeper vendor dependence?
Which compliance or privacy requirements become harder when the model only lives behind someone else's service?

entries.title: Cloudflare
entries.devilDefinition: The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
entries.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
entries.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?
Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings?

entries.title: CodeRabbit
entries.devilDefinition: A reviewer that never gets tired, never gets promoted, and occasionally discovers how lonely correctness can be in a pull request.
entries.plainDefinition: CodeRabbit is an AI code review platform that reviews pull requests and related engineering work, giving automated summaries, findings, suggestions, and workflow assistance across development tools.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI-assisted coding increases the volume and speed of changes, while human review capacity remains stubbornly attached to calendars, context switches, and fatigue.
entries.misuse: It gets misused when teams treat AI review as a replacement for ownership. Automated comments can catch bugs and improve hygiene, but they can also add noise, miss product intent, and create the dangerous feeling that someone else has looked properly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice CodeRabbit means another review layer on the pull request: useful for catching obvious defects, explaining diffs, nudging standards, and giving humans a better starting point rather than an excuse to stop reading.
entries.example: "CodeRabbit caught the missing null check, while the human reviewer still had to notice that the feature solved the wrong problem."
askNext: Which classes of issues does the AI reviewer reliably catch in this repo?
How does the team separate useful findings from review theatre?
Who owns the final merge decision after automated review has spoken?

entries.title: Codex
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who looked at autocomplete and thought, "Fine, but can it also open the repo, run the tests, fix the bug, and stop wasting my afternoon?"
entries.plainDefinition: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent product. Officially, it is designed to read, modify, and run code, either by pairing locally in tools such as the terminal or IDE, or by delegating tasks to isolated cloud sandboxes that can work on repositories in the background.
entries.whyExists: Because plenty of engineering work is not "write me a function". It is inspect the codebase, understand the constraints, make the edit, run the checks, and show the evidence. Codex exists to push beyond suggestion boxes into task execution.
entries.misuse: By talking about Codex as if it were pure autonomous engineering rather than a product built around scoped environments, repo access, tool execution, and human review. The phrase "AI software engineer" tends to arrive several minutes before the conversation about permissions, validation, and merge discipline.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a coding agent that can navigate a repository, edit files, execute tests and linters, explain what it changed, and work either alongside a developer or in a delegated sandbox. The model underneath may change; the durable product question is still whether it can turn a bounded task into reviewable evidence.
entries.example: "The value of Codex was not that it could write code in the abstract. The value was that it could take a bounded task, touch the repo, run the checks, and come back with evidence instead of vibes."
askNext: Is Codex paired locally or running as a delegated cloud task?
What repo, environment, and tool permissions does it have?
Who reviews and validates the work before it lands?

entries.title: Coding Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The software assistant that turned "write the code" into the easy part and "own the consequences" into a fresh management discipline.
entries.plainDefinition: A coding agent is an AI system designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, use documentation or browsers, and carry out multi-step software tasks with some degree of autonomy.
entries.whyExists: Autocomplete was helpful, but it still left humans doing the orchestration: finding the right files, running the tests, checking the docs, fixing the follow-on errors, and keeping the task in memory. Coding agents exist because developers wanted delegation, not merely more enthusiastic tab completion.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that software engineering has become a prompt-shaped clerical job. In reality the category is only impressive when the surrounding repo is legible, the tests say something useful, and an actual engineer is still responsible for the final judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bounded delegation: background code changes, refactors, bug fixes, test runs, and repo chores completed faster than a person would do them by hand, followed by the entirely traditional need for review, context, and rollback plans.
entries.example: "The coding agent cleared three tedious tickets before lunch and generated a fourth, more philosophical ticket in CI."
askNext: What permissions, tools, and feedback loops does the agent actually have?
Which tasks are reliable enough to delegate without constant rescue?
Who reviews the changes when the agent is fast, plausible, and slightly wrong?

entries.title: Computer Use
entries.devilDefinition: The feature where a model is allowed to click around software with the confidence of a junior employee and the supervision requirements of a forklift.
entries.plainDefinition: "Computer use" refers to AI systems that can operate graphical user interfaces by observing screens, deciding actions, and interacting through clicks, typing, scrolling, or other desktop and browser controls.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live inside user interfaces rather than neat APIs. If a system can only answer questions but cannot operate the tools where work actually happens, its usefulness in operations remains politely theoretical.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if seeing a screen and moving a cursor were the same as understanding business process, edge cases, or risk. A demo that closes one support ticket can be described as labour disruption by lunch.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brittle automation with vision, planning, retries, guardrails, and a strong need for scoped permissions. It can save time on repetitive interface work, provided someone is willing to own the mistakes made at machine speed.
entries.example: "Their computer use agent could navigate the vendor portal beautifully, right up to the point a cookie banner became a strategic surprise."
askNext: Which parts of the workflow still require human approval or exception handling?
How does the system recover when the UI changes or a modal appears?
Why is GUI automation the right choice instead of integrating an API?

entries.title: Context Engineering
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
entries.plainDefinition: "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.
entries.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.
entries.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?
How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped?
Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly?

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
entries.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
entries.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?

entries.title: Context Rot
entries.devilDefinition: The slow corruption of a long AI session as every clarification, exception, and half-fixed mistake is preserved with the reverence of scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.
entries.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.
entries.misuse: The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.
entries.example: "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
askNext: Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter?
Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph?
Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it?

entries.title: Context Stuffing
entries.devilDefinition: Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.
entries.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.
entries.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.
entries.example: "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
askNext: Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all?
What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion?
Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text?

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
entries.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
entries.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

entries.title: Context.ai
entries.devilDefinition: A platform for giving agents more of your organisation's actual context, and therefore more of your organisation's actual permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Context.ai is an AI workspace and agent platform built around connecting company systems, capturing workflow context, and running agents against real tasks. Its current pitch emphasises enterprise deployments, connectors, sandboxes, and controlled execution surfaces rather than a generic chat interface.
