entries.misuse: By using LLM as a synonym for AI, intelligence, or product value. An LLM can be the engine inside a useful system, but the acronym by itself explains very little about whether the result is accurate, safe, or worth paying for.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a flexible language-and-code engine that can draft, classify, summarise, translate, search, and bluff, depending on how it is prompted, constrained, and surrounded by everything else.
entries.example: "They said the product was powered by an LLM as though naming the engine answered the question of whether the vehicle was heading anywhere sensible."
askNext: What specific model behaviour matters here beyond the presence of an LLM?
What surrounding retrieval, tooling, or controls make the system useful?
Is the acronym explaining the product, or merely decorating it?

entries.title: LLM-as-a-Judge
entries.devilDefinition: The practice of asking one stochastic witness whether another stochastic witness deserves a trophy.
entries.plainDefinition: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern in which one language model scores, ranks, or compares outputs from another model against a rubric, preference signal, or pairwise choice.
entries.whyExists: Because human evaluation is expensive, slow, and inconveniently unwilling to scale at the pace product teams prefer.
entries.misuse: By treating the judge as neutral, objective, or magically aligned with user value. A model grader can inherit stylistic bias, provider bias, verbosity bias, and a remarkable affection for whatever sounds polished.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it is useful for triage, regression testing, and broad ranking, provided the rubric is clear and the results are anchored to human review on the cases that actually matter.
entries.example: "The model won the internal evals until an actual customer arrived and demonstrated that the judge had confused confidence with usefulness."
askNext: What rubric is the judge following, and who wrote it?
How often do human reviewers disagree with the model's preferences?
Are we using the judge for cheap sorting or pretending it is ground truth?

entries.title: Lovable
entries.devilDefinition: A chat-driven builder for turning "I have an idea" into a working app before anyone asks who will inherit the code.
entries.plainDefinition: Lovable is a product for generating and iterating on web applications from natural-language instructions, with modes for planning, editing code, and increasingly agent-like execution across a project.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many people want software outcomes without local setup, framework literacy, or the traditional rite of discovering three build errors before lunch. Products like this collapse prototyping, editing, and deployment into one highly persuasive loop.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as though describing software and owning software are the same discipline. The tool can absolutely shorten the road to a useful prototype. It cannot repeal the maintenance burden or make architecture an optional hobby.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Lovable usually means faster internal tools, MVPs, marketing sites, and founder demos, plus a fresh opportunity to confuse visible progress with durable engineering.
entries.example: "Lovable got them from idea to live demo in a weekend, which was excellent right up to the moment the product had to become a system."
askNext: Is this a prototype, an internal tool, or something expected to survive real users?
Who understands the resulting code once the initial chat session is over?
Did the tool remove engineering work, or merely defer it to a less festive day?

entries.title: Mac mini
entries.devilDefinition: The little silver box people buy when they want "serious AI infrastructure" without admitting they mostly mean "a neat machine that sits quietly on a desk."
entries.plainDefinition: Mac mini is Apple's compact desktop computer line. In AI conversations, it usually comes up as a relatively tidy, efficient machine for local inference, lightweight automation, development, and always-on orchestration workloads rather than frontier-scale model training. The current M4 and M4 Pro generation is especially relevant because Apple positions it for on-device AI workloads, and the M4 Pro configuration pushes unified memory and I/O far enough to make it a credible local workhorse within limits.
entries.whyExists: Because not every AI workload needs a server rack, and not every ambitious workflow deserves one either.
entries.misuse: By talking about it as if it were either a toy or a supercomputer. It is neither. It is a practical desktop that can be surprisingly capable within its lane and very ordinary outside it.
entries.practicalMeaning: A good box for coding, running local tools, hosting lightweight agents, testing smaller models, or acting as a stable automation node. It is especially attractive when low noise, power efficiency, and desk-friendly deployment matter more than maximum accelerator firepower.
entries.example: "He did not need a DGX. He needed a Mac mini, some discipline, and a better sense of what counted as production."
askNext: Is the workload inference, orchestration, development, or training?
Will it be running one assistant or many workflows?
Is the constraint compute, RAM, storage, or simply operational clarity?

entries.title: Manifest
entries.devilDefinition: A model router for agents whose first autonomous act was apparently choosing the most expensive model available.
entries.plainDefinition: Manifest is an open-source LLM routing layer for personal AI agents, designed to sit behind OpenAI-compatible endpoints and route requests to cheaper or more suitable models based on task complexity and policy.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many agents send every request to the same large model, even when the task is tiny, repetitive, or cheap enough to deserve a less theatrical engine.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for model intelligence rather than routing policy. A router can reduce waste and add fallback behaviour, but it cannot make a weak task definition or poor evaluation strategy suddenly competent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means model-tiering, fallback chains, budget controls, provider abstraction, local or cloud routing, and a sharper view of what each agent request actually costs.
entries.example: "Manifest saved money by routing simple agent chores away from the heroic model, which was a cruel blow to the heroic invoice."
askNext: What signals decide whether a task is simple, complex, or reasoning-heavy?
How are budgets, fallbacks, and provider failures handled?
Does cheaper routing preserve quality on the workloads that actually matter?

entries.title: Manus
entries.devilDefinition: An invite-only demonstration that autonomy feels much more plausible when scarcity does some of the marketing.
entries.plainDefinition: Manus is an agent product associated with multi-step task execution across browsing, research, and software tools, marketed as a system that can carry out longer chains of work than a standard chatbot.
entries.whyExists: The appeal is obvious: people do not merely want better answers, they want finished tasks. A product that promises to browse, compare, draft, click, and return with the work already done sits directly on that desire.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as evidence that general-purpose autonomous agents are effectively solved. In reality, a polished demo reel, a waitlist, and a few successful runs do not abolish brittleness, supervision, or task selection.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a carefully packaged long-running agent workflow with browser actions, tool calls, and enough narrative polish to make ordinary automation feel like a historical turning point.
entries.example: "Manus looked like a digital chief of staff until the workflow left the happy path and became a queue with ambition."
askNext: What permissions, applications, and environments does it actually control?
How often does it recover from website changes, edge cases, or ambiguous instructions without human rescue?
Is the impressive part the autonomy, or the fact that someone selected a task where autonomy photographs well?

entries.title: MCP
entries.devilDefinition: A way of making tools and resources speak a common dialect so the assistant stops needing a bespoke adapter for every cupboard in the house.
entries.plainDefinition: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a protocol for exposing tools, resources, and related capabilities to AI clients in a standardised way, so assistants can discover and use external systems without each integration being entirely custom.
entries.whyExists: AI applications increasingly need access to files, APIs, documentation, databases, prompts, and tools. Without a shared protocol, every client-server pairing becomes its own integration project. MCP exists to reduce that fragmentation.
entries.misuse: It gets spoken about as though a protocol itself makes systems intelligent, secure, or interoperable by magic. It does not. Standardisation helps, but permissions, trust boundaries, authentication, and product design still matter more than the acronym.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means exposing capabilities through a consistent interface so a client can list resources, call tools, and work with external context more systematically. The real value is reducing bespoke glue and making integrations more legible.
entries.example: "MCP did not solve the assistant's judgement problem. It solved the quieter and more useful problem of not writing one-off connectors forever."
askNext: Which tools and resources are exposed through the protocol?
How are authentication, permissions, and auditability handled?
What part of the integration problem is standardised, and what still remains application-specific?

entries.title: Memory Policy
entries.devilDefinition: The quiet constitution deciding what the system remembers, for how long, and which future mistake inherits the privilege.
entries.plainDefinition: Memory policy is the set of rules governing what an AI system stores from interactions, how long it keeps that information, when it retrieves it, and when it must forget or redact it.
entries.whyExists: It exists because continuity is useful and indiscriminate memory is dangerous. Long-running systems can become more helpful by remembering preferences, facts, and prior decisions, but they can also become more wrong, more invasive, and more difficult to debug.
entries.misuse: The term gets reduced to "we have memory now", as if any retained context were inherently intelligent. It also gets used to dress up ordinary logging or vector storage without answering the harder questions about consent, correction, deletion, and sensitive data.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means retention windows, summarisation rules, provenance, retrieval thresholds, redaction filters, user controls, and clear logic for when stale memory should be ignored rather than honoured out of misplaced loyalty.
entries.example: "The assistant felt personal right up until its memory policy allowed one outdated preference to haunt every recommendation for the next three months."
askNext: What information qualifies for memory, and what must never be stored?
How are stale or incorrect memories corrected, deleted, or superseded?
When memory is retrieved, how does the system distinguish relevance from sentimental hoarding?

entries.title: Microsoft AI
entries.devilDefinition: The part of Microsoft tasked with making "Copilot everywhere" sound less like a slogan and more like the natural order of software.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft AI is the organisation Microsoft formed in 2024 to advance Copilot and its consumer AI products and research. It is distinct from Microsoft as a whole, and also distinct from Azure's infrastructure role or OpenAI's position as a model partner.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft needed a named unit for the part of its AI strategy focused on product experience, consumer interfaces, and turning model access into habitual software use.
entries.misuse: By using "Microsoft AI" to mean Microsoft generally, Azure specifically, or OpenAI by implication. The company has several overlapping AI layers; this term refers to the product-and-research organisation built around Copilot and related consumer experiences.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the organisational machinery behind Microsoft's bid to make AI a persistent interface layer across its consumer products, with Copilot as the flagship expression of that strategy.
entries.example: "They said Microsoft AI as if that explained the architecture. In reality it explained the product thesis: put a companion layer in front of as much software as possible."
askNext: Do they mean Microsoft AI, Azure AI infrastructure, or Microsoft's OpenAI partnership?
Is the topic model capability, product interface, or distribution?
How much of the value comes from Copilot branding versus actual workflow integration?

entries.title: Microsoft Copilot
entries.devilDefinition: Software sold as an assistant and deployed as a new layer of management: supervising the machine that was supposed to save you time.
entries.plainDefinition: Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's umbrella brand for AI assistants and generative features across workplace and consumer products, including Microsoft 365, Windows, developer tools, and business software.
entries.whyExists: Because Microsoft wants AI to become the default interface layer across the software people already pay for, turning model access into subscription expansion and habitual workflow presence.
entries.misuse: By using "Copilot" as if it named one coherent product or guaranteed productivity. In reality it is a family resemblance: similar branding spread across many surfaces with uneven usefulness.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means an AI sidebar, a drafting assistant, a meeting-summary machine, a permissions headache, and a fresh round of arguments about whether the licence justifies itself.
entries.example: "The rollout promised a copilot for every employee. What most people got was a new button and one more thing to evaluate before sending an email."
askNext: Which Copilot are we discussing, in which product, with which permissions and data access?
What measurable work improved after adoption rather than merely feeling more modern?
Is this replacing friction, or adding a branded detour to familiar tasks?

entries.title: Midjourney
entries.devilDefinition: A picture factory for turning "I have no visual taste" into "I have a campaign."
entries.plainDefinition: Midjourney is an image-generation product known for stylised visual output, rapid iteration, and strong adoption among designers, marketers, and hobbyists.
entries.whyExists: Because visual ideation is expensive, taste is unevenly distributed, and many people would like concept art, campaign imagery, or mood boards before hiring anyone who can draw.
entries.misuse: By treating it as either the death of design or the same thing as design. It is neither. It is a very powerful image engine whose output still requires selection, direction, and legal and brand judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means mood boards at industrial speed, more options than anyone needed, and a sudden premium on the human ability to reject what merely looks expensive.
entries.example: "Midjourney gave the team one hundred glorious directions, which unfortunately left them needing a designer more than they had an hour earlier."
askNext: Is the goal ideation, final artwork, or merely spectacle for a pitch deck?
Who is making the taste decisions after the model floods the room with options?
What rights, brand constraints, and production steps still sit outside the image generator?

entries.title: MiniMax
entries.devilDefinition: A model vendor whose product surface suggests the future will be multimodal, agentic, and administratively impossible to describe in one sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: MiniMax is an AI company and model platform offering text, speech, video, image, music, file, and agent-facing API capabilities, including official MCP server implementations for some modalities.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model platforms are becoming bundles of capabilities rather than single chat endpoints. Teams increasingly buy text, voice, media generation, tool calling, and integrations from the same vendor shelf.
entries.misuse: It gets reduced to another model name when the operational question is broader: which modality, which API, which latency, which rights, and which integration surface are actually being used?
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor option for multimodal generation, voice and media workflows, agent integrations, and OpenAI or Anthropic SDK-compatible access paths.
entries.example: "MiniMax entered the architecture review as a text model and left carrying voice, video, MCP, and three new procurement questions."
askNext: Which MiniMax capability is in scope: text, speech, video, image, music, or MCP integration?
What are the latency, licensing, data, and moderation constraints for that modality?
Does the unified vendor surface reduce complexity, or merely collect it in one account?

entries.title: Mistral AI
entries.devilDefinition: The French company that managed to make "European frontier AI" sound like a commercial category rather than a wistful policy memo.
entries.plainDefinition: Mistral AI is a French AI company building models, products, and platform services, with a public identity built around efficient frontier systems, enterprise deployment, and a more open posture than many large closed-model competitors.
entries.whyExists: Because the market wanted proof that serious model-building could happen outside the dominant US labs, and because Mistral made strategic use of openness, efficiency, and European identity to become that proof.
entries.misuse: By describing Mistral AI as either purely open-source or purely a geopolitical symbol. It is a commercial model company making selective openness part of its strategy, not a charity for developer ideals.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice for teams that want competitive models, deployment flexibility, open-weight options, and some combination of cost discipline, control, and distance from the biggest US platforms.
entries.example: "They said they wanted Mistral for openness. What they really wanted was optionality, speed, and a way to avoid handing every strategic decision to the usual American suspects."
askNext: Do they want Mistral for model quality, deployment control, openness, or pricing?
Which product do they mean: API models, Le Chat, enterprise platform, or open-weight releases?
Is the attraction technical fit or strategic independence?

entries.title: Mixture of Experts
entries.devilDefinition: An architecture for having enormous parameter counts without insisting every token use the whole cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: A mixture-of-experts model is a neural architecture in which a routing mechanism activates only a subset of specialised sub-networks for each token or input, allowing higher total capacity without using all parameters on every pass.
entries.whyExists: Because model builders want more capacity and performance without paying the full compute cost of dense models at every step. Sparse activation offers a way to scale capability more economically, at least in theory.
entries.misuse: By treating "experts" as if the model contains little professors with stable domains of knowledge. It is an optimisation strategy, not a panel discussion. The routing can help a great deal, but it does not grant wisdom by committee.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means better parameter-efficiency on paper, more complex training and serving behaviour, routing overhead, and a fresh set of engineering questions about load balancing, latency spikes, and uneven quality.
entries.example: "The model was a mixture of experts, which sounded philosophical until the serving team had to explain the routing bottlenecks."
askNext: How many experts exist, and how many are active per token?
What did sparse routing improve relative to a dense baseline at the same cost?
Did the architecture move the bottleneck from compute to systems complexity?

entries.title: Model Collapse
entries.devilDefinition: The fear that if the machines keep learning from one another's homework, the class average will eventually become the curriculum.
entries.plainDefinition: Model collapse is the hypothesised degradation that can occur when models are trained repeatedly on synthetic or model-generated data without enough fresh, high-quality human data to preserve diversity and fidelity.
entries.whyExists: Because synthetic data is useful, cheap, and increasingly tempting at scale, but feedback loops can distort distributions, wash out edge cases, and quietly teach systems to imitate their own errors.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a guaranteed apocalypse or as proof that synthetic data is worthless. Synthetic data can be valuable. The concern is about unmanaged feedback loops, weak provenance, and replacing judgement with volume.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means teams need stronger data lineage, better curation, and honest evaluation before they let generated material dominate the training mix just because it is abundant and conveniently machine-readable.
entries.example: "The lab was not worried about synthetic data in principle. It was worried about model collapse from feeding the next system a buffet of the previous system's bad habits."
askNext: How much of the training mix is synthetic, and how is its quality measured?
What prevents generated noise from becoming self-confirming doctrine?
Are we preserving human-created edge cases or optimising them out of existence?

entries.title: Model Garden
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud menu where every model is arranged like a tasteful orchard and billed like enterprise software.
entries.plainDefinition: Model garden is a vendor term for a managed catalogue of models that customers can browse, provision, fine-tune, and call through a cloud platform.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers want more than one model, clouds want to be the place where that choice gets made, and nobody enjoys integrating a fresh API every time the leaderboard twitches.
entries.misuse: By making model access sound like curation solved the hard part. A catalogue is useful. It does not answer which model is trustworthy, economical, regionally available, or appropriate for a real workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement-and-deployment layer with billing, quotas, IAM, regions, logging, and just enough abstraction to turn model shopping into infrastructure policy.
entries.example: "The model garden looked abundant until the architecture review reduced it to the three options legal, security, and latency could tolerate."
askNext: Which models are actually production-ready for our constraints?
What vendor lock-in sits behind the pleasant catalogue language?
Are we choosing a model, or quietly choosing an entire cloud relationship?

entries.title: Model Router
entries.devilDefinition: The switchboard that promises the right model for every job and occasionally adds a thrilling new place for policy mistakes to hide.
entries.plainDefinition: A model router is a software layer that selects among multiple models or providers for a given request based on rules or signals such as task type, latency, cost, availability, policy, or fallback behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Because no single model is best at everything, outages happen, prices move, and product teams would prefer not to hard-code every bet into the application layer.
entries.misuse: By marketing routing itself as intelligence, or by implying that a multi-model stack is automatically well evaluated. A router can express good operational judgement. It can also just spread uncertainty across more vendors.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means heuristics, policy rules, retries, provider abstractions, observability, and a permanent negotiation between quality, speed, and the monthly bill.
entries.example: "The model router kept the app online during the outage, then introduced a fresh round of debugging because two providers interpreted the same tool schema differently."
askNext: What signals drive routing decisions, and were they benchmarked on real workloads?
What is the fallback policy when the preferred model fails or drifts?
Does the router reduce operational risk, or merely distribute it?

entries.title: Multimodal
entries.devilDefinition: Able to juggle text, images, audio, or video, which sounds far more coherent than the reality of several data types sharing one product team.
entries.plainDefinition: Multimodal refers to models or systems that can process, generate, or jointly reason over more than one data modality, such as text, image, audio, or video.
entries.whyExists: Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.
entries.misuse: It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.
entries.example: "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
askNext: Which modalities can the system actually handle in production?
Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome?
How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?

entries.title: Mustafa Suleyman
entries.devilDefinition: The fluent translator of machine intelligence into product language for people who would rather adopt a companion than study a model card.
entries.plainDefinition: Mustafa Suleyman is the CEO of Microsoft AI and a prominent founder-operator associated with productising advanced AI into mass-market interfaces, assistants, and companion-style experiences.
entries.whyExists: Because AI is not only a research contest. It is also a packaging contest, an interface contest, and a trust contest. Suleyman's name often marks the point where the conversation shifts from raw capability to how intelligence is turned into habit.
entries.misuse: By treating "companion" language as either obviously profound or obviously absurd. It is better understood as a serious product thesis about persistence, personalisation, convenience, and software that wants to sit between the user and everything else.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Mustafa Suleyman, they usually mean AI as a persistent product layer: more assistant, less query box; more relationship, less transaction.
entries.example: "The roadmap had gone full Mustafa: less tool, more companion, with enough memory and polish to make dependence sound like convenience."
askNext: Is this about interface design, product strategy, or model capability?
Does the vision depend on trust, habit, or lock-in?
Are we building a workflow tool, a companion, or a polite way to blur the line?

entries.title: Nemotron 3
entries.devilDefinition: NVIDIA's model family for anyone who suspected the GPU company might also enjoy owning more of what runs on the GPUs.
entries.plainDefinition: Nemotron 3 is NVIDIA's open model family with Nano, Super, and Ultra variants positioned for agentic, reasoning, coding, and conversational workloads.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model vendors increasingly want families that span different cost and performance points, especially for agent workloads where serving efficiency and deployment control matter as much as leaderboard theatre.
entries.misuse: It gets flattened into "NVIDIA has a model" as though all variants, licences, serving choices, and agent benchmarks collapse into one tidy procurement noun.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating an NVIDIA-backed open model family for agentic workloads, often alongside questions about hardware fit, NIM deployment, open-weight hosting, and price-performance.
entries.example: "Nemotron 3 entered the shortlist because the team wanted an open model that made sense on the hardware strategy already approved by finance."
askNext: Which Nemotron 3 variant are we testing: Nano, Super, or Ultra?
Are we choosing it for agent capability, serving economics, openness, or NVIDIA stack alignment?
How does it perform on our own workflows rather than the family brochure?

entries.title: Neocloud
entries.devilDefinition: A cloud provider whose most disruptive feature is often discovering that scarce Nvidia hardware can be resold with excellent posture.
entries.plainDefinition: "Neocloud" is an informal label for newer cloud providers focused on AI infrastructure, especially GPU capacity for training and inference, rather than broad general-purpose enterprise cloud offerings.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because demand for accelerators outran easy supply, while many teams wanted alternatives to the hyperscalers on price, access, support, geography, or simple willingness to answer the phone.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever specialised capacity is treated as a permanent moat. Owning or brokering GPUs can be valuable. It does not automatically solve networking, reliability, data governance, procurement, or the small inconvenience of debt.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a provider that can get you machines faster, sometimes cheaper, sometimes with better human support, in exchange for narrower platform breadth and a closer relationship with the physics of hardware finance.
entries.example: "The neocloud pitch was compelling right up to the moment finance noticed the business model involved both GPUs and optimism."
askNext: Are we choosing this provider for price, access, support, location, or all four?
What happens to reliability, networking, and compliance once we leave the big clouds?
Is this a real platform advantage or an opportunistic position in a supply shortage?

entries.title: NotebookLM
entries.devilDefinition: The unusually sensible AI product that became popular by admitting you should probably start with your own documents.
entries.plainDefinition: NotebookLM is Google's source-grounded research and note-taking product for uploading or linking material, asking questions about it, generating summaries, and producing derivative outputs such as briefs or audio overviews.
entries.whyExists: Most knowledge work does not need an omniscient machine performing personality. It needs help digesting a bounded set of files, links, transcripts, slides, or notes without wandering off into generic internet mush.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for a citation guarantee or a truth engine. Grounding in supplied sources materially helps, but the system can still overstate, misconnect, or present generated synthesis with more poise than certainty.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice NotebookLM means a polished retrieval-and-synthesis interface for source packs: useful for study guides, research briefings, interview prep, internal manuals, and turning document piles into something a human can survive.
entries.example: "NotebookLM did not make the report magically correct, but it did make the document swamp considerably less vindictive."
askNext: Which sources are actually in the notebook, and which claims come from outside them?
Is the value in source-grounded synthesis, or are we quietly expecting general web research as well?
Would a document search tool be enough, or do we genuinely need generated structure on top?

entries.title: NPU
entries.devilDefinition: The dedicated silicon every device launch now invokes to prove the future has arrived locally, efficiently, and preferably in this quarter's hardware cycle.
entries.plainDefinition: An NPU is a specialised processor designed to accelerate machine-learning workloads, especially on-device inference tasks such as speech, vision, transcription, or smaller language-model features.
entries.whyExists: Because CPUs and GPUs are not always the best fit for constant local inference, and device makers want better performance per watt for AI features that cannot afford cloud latency or cloud disclosure.
entries.misuse: By treating the presence of an NPU as evidence that meaningful AI features exist, or by presenting the acronym as a product benefit without saying what models fit, what latency improves, or what the software stack can actually use.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice an NPU determines which features can run on-device, how responsive and private they can be, and whether "AI PC" or "AI phone" means a new capability or merely a fresh sticker on the box.
entries.example: "The keynote celebrated the NPU at length, which was useful because the list of software features still fit comfortably on one slide."
askNext: Which models or tasks actually run on the NPU today?
What latency, battery, or privacy gains are measurable rather than implied?
Is the developer stack mature enough to use the hardware well?

entries.title: NVIDIA
entries.devilDefinition: A chip company that became the closest thing the AI boom has to a central bank, except louder and with better leather jackets.
entries.plainDefinition: NVIDIA is a computing platform company best known in AI for GPUs, accelerated computing systems, and the broader hardware-software stack used to train and serve many modern AI models.
entries.whyExists: Because AI keeps pretending to be abstract until someone has to pay for the compute. NVIDIA matters because the current boom runs on its chips, systems, software ecosystem, and the procurement gravity that follows them.
entries.misuse: By using NVIDIA as shorthand for AI itself. That exaggerates the company and erases the rest of the stack, but it does capture where a great deal of pricing power and strategic leverage currently sits.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means GPUs, DGX-class systems, CUDA-shaped ecosystems, inference clusters, and the hardware economics under both frontier training and production deployment.
entries.example: "The board wanted an AI strategy. The operations team, less poetically, wanted to know how much of it would end up being tribute to NVIDIA."
askNext: Is this a chip discussion, a systems discussion, or a software-ecosystem discussion?
Are we bottlenecked by compute, cost, availability, or habit?
Could another stack do the job, or are we already too CUDA-shaped to be honest about the answer?

entries.title: NVIDIA NIM
entries.devilDefinition: NVIDIA's reminder that once everyone wants models in production, the pick-and-shovel business starts selling neatly boxed shovels.
entries.plainDefinition: NVIDIA NIM is a set of microservices and API surfaces for deploying, serving, and trying foundation models across NVIDIA's AI stack, including hosted NIM APIs exposed through build.nvidia.com.
entries.whyExists: It exists because serving models is not just a model choice. Teams also need runtimes, endpoints, containers, security updates, deployment patterns, and enough operational packaging to make inference feel less handmade.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if the presence of an official endpoint solves model selection, quality evaluation, latency budgets, or vendor lock-in. A production-grade serving wrapper still serves whatever decision you made inside it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means easier access to NVIDIA-optimised model serving, hosted trials, and deployment recipes for teams already thinking in GPUs, data centres, clouds, and enterprise support contracts.
entries.example: "They tried the model through NVIDIA NIM first, which was sensible; they then called the working API call a platform strategy, which was less so."
askNext: Are we using NIM as a hosted API, a deployable microservice, or a reference runtime?
Which model, hardware target, latency budget, and data-handling requirements matter here?
Does NIM simplify deployment enough to justify the surrounding NVIDIA dependency?

entries.title: Ollama
entries.devilDefinition: The software that persuaded a great many people to download a model locally and call the resulting fan noise a privacy strategy.
entries.plainDefinition: Ollama is a tool and runtime for downloading, serving, and interacting with local AI models through a straightforward CLI and API on a developer machine or private system.
entries.whyExists: Running local models used to feel more like a weekend project than a developer workflow. Ollama exists because many teams wanted a simple way to try local inference, private prototypes, and offline model use without assembling the whole stack by hand.
entries.misuse: It gets mistaken for a complete production strategy or proof that local inference is automatically secure, cheap, or enterprise-ready. Pulling a model locally is helpful. It does not abolish memory limits, observability, scaling, or the possibility that the chosen model is merely mediocre in private.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means convenient local model packaging for experiments, prototypes, internal tools, and developer workflows. It is especially useful for reducing friction around self-hosted testing, less useful as a substitute for a real serving platform once traffic, governance, or uptime begin to matter.
entries.example: "They rolled the demo on Ollama because the requirement was a private proof of concept, not a treatise on distributed inference."
askNext: Which model and quantisation are we actually running?
Is local inference solving privacy, latency, cost, or merely setup friction?
Do we need a developer runtime, or do we really need a production serving stack?

entries.title: One-Person Unicorn
entries.devilDefinition: The recurring fantasy that one founder, several agents, and a cloud invoice can replace an organisation and still count as a company instead of an episode.
entries.plainDefinition: The "one-person unicorn" is the idea that AI tools and automation could let a single founder build and run a company worth a billion dollars with little or no traditional staff.
entries.whyExists: It exists because AI has genuinely reduced the cost of coding, design, support, research, and back-office work enough for solo founders to reach farther than they could a few years ago. That makes the old dream of extreme leverage newly discussable and newly distortive.
entries.misuse: The term gets used as if headcount were simply a regrettable inefficiency. It understates the boring things companies are still made of: trust, sales, operations, compliance, customer support, and the general inconvenience of reality needing more than one pair of eyes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means smaller teams doing more, solo founders getting to revenue faster, and investors testing how much organisational fragility they can rename as leverage.
entries.example: "The one-person unicorn thesis sounds marvellous until the founder discovers that support, tax, contracts, and incident response still decline to become vibes."
askNext: Which parts of the company are actually automated, and which parts are being silently done by contractors, software, or wishful thinking?
Does the business model scale without a team, or only the demo?
What breaks first when a supposedly solo company meets regulation, churn, or enterprise procurement?

entries.title: Open Weight
entries.devilDefinition: The compromise term for when a company wants the glow of openness without the administrative burden of actually being open source.
entries.plainDefinition: An open-weight model is one whose trained parameters are released for others to download and run, even if the training code, data, licences, or usage rights remain restricted.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because there is a meaningful difference between sharing a model's weights and sharing the full ingredients behind it. The industry needed language for that middle ground, if only to avoid calling everything open source with a straight face.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever "open weight" is presented as equivalent to full transparency, unrestricted reuse, or community governance. A downloadable checkpoint can still arrive with significant legal, technical, and commercial strings attached.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you can run or fine-tune the model yourself, but you may not know precisely how it was trained, what data shaped it, or what terms govern your most lucrative use case.
entries.example: "The vendor called it open weight, which was accurate enough if you ignored the licence, the data secrecy, and the very expensive preferred hosting arrangement."
askNext: What exactly has been released besides the checkpoint?
What restrictions apply to commercial use, redistribution, or modification?
Can the model be audited meaningfully, or only downloaded?

entries.title: OpenAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name frequently used as shorthand for an app, a model family, an API, a policy debate, and sometimes the entire industry when people cannot be bothered to separate nouns.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenAI is a company. It builds and sells AI products and services, including model APIs and end-user products such as ChatGPT. The company name is distinct from the names of its individual models and products.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because company, product, and model are constantly collapsed into one another. If you do not separate them, conversations about performance, pricing, safety, procurement, and market position become foggy very quickly.
entries.misuse: People say "OpenAI said this" when they mean ChatGPT produced a reply, or "we use OpenAI" when they actually use a specific API model through a specific product flow. The confusion sounds minor until accuracy, vendor risk, or budgeting starts mattering.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a commercial vendor relationship. You are choosing a supplier, a set of models, platform terms, product constraints, and an evolving roadmap. The most important question is usually not whether OpenAI exists, but which exact OpenAI product or model you mean.
entries.example: "The board memo said the company was 'using OpenAI', which turned out to mean one customer-service workflow calling a single model through the API with very strict prompt scaffolding."
askNext: Do you mean the company, the API platform, ChatGPT, or a specific model?
What dependency does the product have on this vendor: model quality, tooling, pricing, or distribution?
What is the fallback plan if the model, terms, or costs change?

entries.title: OpenClaw
entries.devilDefinition: An AI assistant for people who looked at a chatbot and thought, "Nice, but can it also touch my files, my browser, my messages, and my calendar?"
entries.plainDefinition: OpenClaw is a self-hosted personal AI assistant and gateway platform designed to do things, not just chat. Its own docs describe it as an assistant you run on your own devices, replying through surfaces such as Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, iMessage, Slack, and WebChat while orchestrating tools, memory, and workflows through an always-on gateway.
entries.whyExists: Because the market got bored of systems that only generate text and started demanding systems that can actually execute tasks.
entries.misuse: By treating any autonomous workflow as magic, or by forgetting that giving an AI broad permissions turns "helpful assistant" into "possible incident". The interesting part is not that it can act. The interesting part is how narrowly, visibly, and safely it acts.
entries.practicalMeaning: A software layer that connects models to tools, interfaces, and actions so that the system can perform useful work across apps and services. In practice it is closer to an orchestration-and-gateway stack than a free-floating digital butler.
entries.example: "OpenClaw is what people reach for when they want the AI to stop explaining the inbox and start clearing it."
askNext: What tools does it have access to?
What permissions does it require?
Is it mostly chat, mostly automation, or truly operational?
Where does the human stay in the loop?

entries.title: OpenCode
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who want the assistant close to the repo, not trapped inside a subscription-shaped cathedral.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent for terminal, IDE, and desktop workflows, designed to work with multiple model providers and help developers inspect, edit, and operate on codebases.
entries.whyExists: It exists because coding agents moved from autocomplete into delegated repo work, and developers wanted tools that were model-flexible, scriptable, local-friendly, and less tied to a single vendor's product surface.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that openness alone creates reliability. An open agent can still edit the wrong file, misunderstand the task, or spend tokens with the confidence of any other agent wearing better governance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a repo-facing coding agent that can run near a developer's existing workflow, connect to different model providers, and trade polished platform control for flexibility and inspectability.
entries.example: "OpenCode helped the team move faster, then politely reminded them that open source does not abolish code review."
askNext: Which model provider, permissions, and repo tools does OpenCode have access to?
Does the local workflow improve reviewability, or simply make mistakes arrive faster?
What tasks are safe to delegate, and which still need a human at the keyboard?

entries.title: OpenRouter
entries.devilDefinition: A toll road for developers who want access to every model and one more month before choosing a real dependency.
entries.plainDefinition: OpenRouter is a gateway service that exposes many AI models behind a unified API, letting developers compare providers, route traffic, and manage access through one integration layer.
entries.whyExists: Because the model market is fragmented, provider APIs differ, and teams prefer one contract when they are still shopping for capability, price, latency, or fallback options.
entries.misuse: By implying that abstraction removes vendor risk rather than merely relocating it. A router can hide some differences. It cannot abolish model quirks, pricing shocks, policy changes, or provider outages.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a gateway for multi-model experimentation, centralised billing, fallback logic, and the comforting illusion that indecision has become architecture.
entries.example: "They integrated OpenRouter so product could compare five models in a week and postpone commitment with unusual professionalism."
askNext: Which provider-specific behaviours still leak through the abstraction?
Is the router saving real engineering time or merely extending evaluation limbo?
What happens to cost control, observability, and data handling once another layer sits in the middle?

entries.title: Orchestration
entries.devilDefinition: The noble art of arranging prompts, tools, retries, rules, and side effects so that a demo looks like intelligence rather than plumbing.
entries.plainDefinition: Orchestration is the software layer that coordinates model calls, tool use, retrieval, validation, branching logic, retries, and state across a workflow. It determines how the surrounding system manages the model, not what the model intrinsically knows.
entries.whyExists: Modern AI products are rarely a single model call. They are systems. Orchestration exists because useful behaviour often depends on ordering steps, handling failures, and deciding when to ask a model versus when to run conventional software.
entries.misuse: The term gets abused when simple glue code is dressed up as autonomous reasoning. Sometimes orchestration is just sensible application logic wearing a tuxedo because "if statements and APIs" sounded too ordinary for the pitch deck.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a chain of decisions around prompts, routing, tool permissions, schema checks, retrieval steps, model selection, human approvals, and error handling. Good orchestration makes systems reliable. Bad orchestration makes them busy.
entries.example: "The impressive part was not the model; it was the orchestration layer that knew when to search the docs, when to call billing, and when to stop pretending confidence."
askNext: What steps are being orchestrated, and which of them actually require a model?
Where does state live between steps?
What happens when a tool call fails, returns nonsense, or arrives too late?

entries.title: Output Laundering
entries.devilDefinition: The corporate ritual of running your opinion through a machine so it returns wearing objectivity.
entries.plainDefinition: The act of using AI output to make a human preference, hunch, or decision appear more neutral, data-driven, or authoritative than it really is.
entries.whyExists: Because organisations enjoy the prestige of algorithmic distance. An answer feels less political, less personal, and less blameworthy once a system is seen to have produced it.
entries.misuse: By pretending the model independently discovered the conclusion when the framing, source selection, or prompt design all but guaranteed it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a machine-generated report, ranking, or recommendation being used to formalise a decision management already wanted to make.
entries.example: "They did not want the model's judgement. They wanted output laundering: a printable reason to do what they had already decided."
askNext: Who framed the problem, selected the sources, and wrote the prompt?
Would the conclusion have surprised anyone involved?
Is the system informing the decision or deodorising it?

entries.title: Paper
entries.devilDefinition: A design canvas for people who looked at the wall between mockup and code and decided the wall should have fewer feelings.
entries.plainDefinition: Paper is a design platform built around a web-native canvas, where teams can design, share, and ship interface work with code, data, and agent workflows closer to the same surface.
entries.whyExists: It exists because visual design, prototype review, implementation, and AI-assisted iteration often happen in separate tools that translate badly into one another. A web-standard canvas tries to make the artefact more portable.
entries.misuse: It can be oversold as if an HTML/CSS-aware canvas eliminates product design judgement, engineering taste, or the awkward final mile where a pretty surface has to survive real application state.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a design space aimed at reducing handoff loss: designers, agents, code, and web output operate closer together, so a prototype has a better chance of becoming implementation material rather than a decorative screenshot.
entries.example: "They used Paper because the agent could reason over something closer to the web page they actually needed, not a museum specimen of a future interface."
askNext: Is Paper being used as the source of truth, a prototyping layer, or a bridge into code?
How much of the generated output survives real responsive states and product data?
Where does design review happen once agents can also edit the canvas?

entries.title: Perplexity
entries.devilDefinition: A search product for people who found ten blue links too democratic and preferred one confident stranger.
entries.plainDefinition: Perplexity is an AI-first search and answer product that combines web retrieval, synthesis, and cited responses in a chat-style interface.
entries.whyExists: Because conventional search makes users do the synthesis themselves, while chat products taught people to expect a direct answer even when the subject deserved more caution.
entries.misuse: By treating a cited answer as the same thing as a trustworthy answer. Links help. They do not absolve the reader of checking whether the system chose sensible sources or summarised them honestly.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means faster reconnaissance on a topic, fewer tabs, and a subtle transfer of editorial control from the user to the answer engine.
entries.example: "Perplexity saved him half an hour of clicking, then cost him ten minutes of verifying that the neat summary had not quietly simplified the important part away."
askNext: Which sources did it trust first, and why?
Is the answer good because it is accurate or because it is convenient?
Would you notice if it omitted the most inconvenient source?

entries.title: Personal Intelligence
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your assistant becomes more useful once it is permitted a supervised rummage through your digital life.
entries.plainDefinition: Personal Intelligence is Google's feature for connecting apps like Gmail and Photos to Gemini or AI Mode so the system can answer with more personal context.
entries.whyExists: Generic assistants plateau quickly. Connected assistants can answer questions that only make sense against your own history, purchases, plans, and messages. The term exists because context is now a product feature, not merely a prompt length problem.
entries.misuse: It is framed as pure helpfulness when the real proposition is intimacy with guardrails. Opt-in controls matter, but the underlying bargain is still clear: give the system more of your life and hope the convenience is worth the access.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means personalised itineraries, recommendations, memory-like assistance across Google products, and a new set of design and trust questions about permissions, boundaries, and how much context people really want their assistant to hold.
entries.example: "Personal Intelligence made the travel plan far more relevant, which was lovely right up until everyone remembered what relevance had to be fed."
askNext: Which apps are connected, and what data is actually in play?
Is the value coming from better answers, or from making the assistant harder to leave?
What happens to trust once convenience starts depending on deeper access?

entries.title: Physical AI
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of robotics for an era in which software valuations discovered they might need a body after all.
entries.plainDefinition: "Physical AI" is a broad industry label for applying modern AI systems to embodied machines such as robots, vehicles, and industrial equipment operating in real environments.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because language and image models do not move boxes, navigate warehouses, or survive factory floors by themselves. Embodied systems need perception, control, planning, simulation, and safety engineering tied to physical constraints.
entries.misuse: It is misused whenever a polished robot demo is treated as evidence that deployment is near. Hardware, safety, reliability, maintenance, and integration remain stubbornly unimpressed by keynote vocabulary.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means expensive sensor stacks, simulation pipelines, teleoperation fallbacks, long deployment cycles, and a great deal of engineering dedicated to the fact that the physical world does not accept almost right.
entries.example: "The physical AI roadmap sounded transformative until operations asked who would service the fleet after the first enthusiastic collision."
askNext: What part of the system is actually learned, and what part is conventional robotics?
How does the system fail in messy real environments rather than curated demos?
What are the safety, maintenance, and integration costs after the pilot ends?

entries.title: Pieter Levels
entries.devilDefinition: The indie-hacker folk hero who makes ordinary organisations look less underpowered than overexcused.
entries.plainDefinition: Pieter Levels is an indie maker known for products such as Nomad List and Remote OK and, in contemporary AI culture, for representing the argument that one person with distribution, taste, and AI tooling can build more than many small teams would care to admit.
entries.whyExists: Because some names stop referring only to a person and start referring to a method. Invoking Levels usually means shipping in public, bootstrapping, keeping teams tiny, and treating software as something to release before the deck is finished apologising.
entries.misuse: By turning an unusual operator with an audience into a universal organisational theory. His style is instructive, but it does not erase survivorship bias, domain complexity, support work, or the awkward truth that most founders are not Pieter Levels with a timeline full of customers.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Levels usually means solo-founder leverage, fast iteration, blunt market testing, and a belief that AI tools amplify people who already know how to ship, write, and sell.
entries.example: "The moment someone invoked @levelsio, the roadmap became less a plan than an accusation that the company might simply have too many meetings."
askNext: Are they borrowing his execution style, or just his mythology?
Is the lesson about distribution, speed, team size, or public accountability?
Would this approach still hold once the product had enterprise customers, compliance, or a support queue?

entries.title: Pilot Purgatory
entries.devilDefinition: The budgetary afterlife in which every firm proves its boldness by never quite deploying anything.
entries.plainDefinition: A recurring organisational state in which AI initiatives remain stuck in trials, proofs of concept, and limited rollouts without becoming trusted, scaled operations.
entries.whyExists: Because demos are easier than deployment, sponsors like novelty more than process change, and real adoption usually requires data access, security review, staff enablement, governance, and someone to own the outcome.
entries.misuse: By calling endless experimentation strategy. A pilot is a learning phase. It becomes purgatory when the organisation wants the optics of innovation without paying the price of commitment.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means many vendor meetings, a few polished demos, no durable workflow change, and a graveyard of licences nobody remembers approving.
entries.example: "The company had twelve AI pilots and one AI workflow in production. Pilot purgatory was not a risk; it was the operating model."
askNext: What concrete condition must be met for this pilot to graduate or die?
Who owns the workflow after the novelty budget runs out?
What operational change is the organisation avoiding by calling this an experiment?

entries.title: Post-Training
entries.devilDefinition: All the work done after pretraining so the base model can stop sounding raw, evasive, or commercially inconvenient.
entries.plainDefinition: Post-training is the phase after base-model pretraining where a model is further adapted through methods such as supervised fine-tuning, preference optimisation, safety tuning, distillation, tool-use training, or reasoning-focused tuning.
entries.whyExists: Because a pretrained model may have broad knowledge yet still be poor at following instructions, using tools, refusing unsafe requests, formatting outputs, or behaving in ways customers and policy teams can live with.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a minor polish step. In modern systems, post-training often shapes the behaviour users actually experience. The base model matters. So does everything done afterwards to civilise it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means expensive data curation, human or synthetic preference signals, eval loops, reward design, refusal policies, task-specific tuning, and long arguments about whether capability gains came from the base model or the scaffolding layered on top.
entries.example: "The pretraining got the headlines. The post-training taught the model how to survive product management."
askNext: Which behaviours come from the base model, and which were imposed during post-training?
What data or feedback signals shaped instruction following, refusals, and tool behaviour?
Did post-training improve real task quality or merely make the model easier to demo?

entries.title: Project Glasswing
entries.devilDefinition: A security initiative built on the now-plausible fear that the same model helping defenders today could industrialise attackers tomorrow.
entries.plainDefinition: Project Glasswing is Anthropic's programme for giving selected partners early access to Claude Mythos Preview to find and fix vulnerabilities in critical software and infrastructure.
entries.whyExists: Models have become strong enough at code and systems reasoning that cybersecurity is no longer just another benchmark story. If a model can inspect, understand, and alter complex software unusually well, defence and offence both start moving faster.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as proof that cyber doom is already here or as proof that gated access itself counts as a sufficient safety strategy. Neither conclusion is especially adult. Restricted preview buys time. It does not settle the broader governance problem.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a closed partner programme, usage credits, system cards, research-preview language, and a highly managed attempt to steer frontier capability toward defensive work before the surrounding market catches up in less polite ways.
entries.example: "Project Glasswing was the moment the industry stopped speaking about coding gains in the abstract and started sounding like it had met a regulator."
askNext: What capability is being gated here, and how much of it is genuinely new?
Who gets access first, and what public benefit follows from that privilege?
Is this a real defensive programme, or an unusually sober product launch?

entries.title: Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: A politely worded attempt to steer a probability machine into behaving as if it has read the brief and cares.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt is the input text, and sometimes structured context, provided to a model in order to shape its output. In LLM systems this can include instructions, examples, retrieved documents, tool results, formatting rules, and the user's actual request.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the way you frame input materially affects output. Language models do not simply receive a question. They respond to the entire text context they are given.
entries.misuse: It gets treated either as wizardry or as trivia. Both are wrong. Prompt quality matters, but prompting is not a substitute for missing product design, missing data, or missing control logic. A good prompt cannot rescue a bad system forever.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the assembled context sent to the model: system instructions, user text, retrieved snippets, examples, tool schemas, output rules, and maybe the remnants of last week's prompt experiments still haunting production.
entries.example: "The issue was not that the model was weak; the prompt was half policy manual, half legal disclaimer, and no longer resembled a usable instruction."
askNext: What exact context is being sent to the model?
Which parts of the prompt are fixed system behaviour versus user input?
Is the prompt compensating for a missing tool, missing retrieval step, or missing product rule?

entries.title: Prompt Architecture
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to turn a growing pile of instructions into something more defensible than folklore and copy-paste.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt architecture is the deliberate structure of system prompts, task instructions, examples, tool schemas, retrieved context, and output constraints across a model-driven workflow.
entries.whyExists: It exists because complex AI systems no longer send one neat instruction and hope for the best. They assemble multiple layers of context, and the order, boundaries, and ownership of those layers affect reliability more than anyone wanted to admit in the early prompt-engineering era.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a grand title for very long prompts and very little discipline. Sometimes the work really is architectural. Sometimes the phrase is just a more respectable way of saying nobody wants to untangle the string anymore.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating stable policy from task-level instructions, defining insertion points for retrieval and tool results, deciding what gets versioned, and preventing one helpful addition after another from turning the whole prompt into legal compost.
entries.example: "Their prompt architecture improved the moment they stopped letting every new requirement attach itself to the same immortal system message."
askNext: Which parts of the prompt are fixed policy, and which parts are task-specific assembly?
Where do retrieved context, examples, and tool outputs enter the stack?
What happens when you remove one section: collapse, improvement, or merely a shorter superstition?

entries.title: Prompt Caching
entries.devilDefinition: The serving trick where you pay full price once so the model can stop rereading your sacred system prompt on every request.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt caching is a technique where a provider reuses the already processed prefix of repeated prompts, reducing latency and input cost when the beginning of the request stays the same.
entries.whyExists: It exists because long instructions, tool schemas, examples, and reference context are expensive to process from scratch every time. If large parts of a prompt repeat, caching lets systems avoid doing the same preparatory work over and over.
entries.misuse: It gets pitched as free speed or magical memory. In reality it only helps when prompts share the right prefix, the provider's cache keeps it warm, and the traffic pattern is disciplined enough to hit it again before it expires.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means putting stable instructions first, volatile user data later, watching cache hit rates, and discovering that prompt order has quietly become a finance decision.
entries.example: "Prompt caching finally started paying off once they stopped putting the customer-specific chaos before the reusable instructions."
askNext: Which part of the prompt is actually stable enough to be reused?
How long do cache hits survive on this provider before they quietly disappear?
Are you measuring real latency and cost gains, or just admiring the idea of efficiency?

entries.title: Prompt Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who discovered that giving clearer instructions becomes prestigious once the recipient is nonhuman.
entries.plainDefinition: A person who specialises in shaping prompts, examples, constraints, and interaction patterns to produce better results from AI systems.
entries.whyExists: Because generated output depends heavily on framing. Clear instructions, useful examples, and well-chosen constraints often matter more than people expect.
entries.misuse: By inflating careful prompting into a mystical profession or pretending it removes the need for domain expertise, testing, and workflow design.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means part writer, part editor, part product thinker: someone trying to make a probabilistic system behave consistently enough for actual use.
entries.example: "They hired a prompt engineer. What they really needed was someone to specify the task clearly enough that the rest of the team would stop contradicting itself."
askNext: Are they improving prompts, examples, system constraints, or the workflow around them?
Can the approach produce repeatable gains, or is it artisanal luck with version numbers?
Would the task improve just as much if the humans were clearer with one another?

entries.title: Prompt Firewall
entries.devilDefinition: A security product that promises to stand between your model and human creativity, a famously restful place to stand.
entries.plainDefinition: A prompt firewall is a protective layer that inspects model inputs, outputs, tool calls, or retrieved content for policy violations, prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe instructions, and other risky patterns.
entries.whyExists: AI systems accept natural language from users, documents, tools, and websites. That makes ordinary application boundaries porous, so teams need checks that understand text, intent, context, and the strange confidence of a poisoned instruction.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as a complete shield. A firewall can reduce known classes of risk, but it cannot replace threat modelling, least-privilege tools, red-team testing, or the habit of not handing production powers to a chatbot with a clipboard.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means classifiers, rules, allowlists, deny lists, logging, human review paths, and careful placement around retrieval and tool execution. The hard part is blocking the right things without turning the product into a polite refusal machine.
entries.example: "The prompt firewall caught the obvious injection and missed the support document that asked the agent to email payroll with theatrical confidence."
askNext: Where is the firewall placed: before retrieval, after retrieval, before tools, after tools, or merely in the sales deck?
What happens when the firewall is unsure?
Are blocked requests audited, or are they just counted until the dashboard feels serious?

entries.title: Prompt Injection
entries.devilDefinition: The trick of slipping a second brief into the machine and watching it forget who is supposed to be in charge.
entries.plainDefinition: Prompt injection is a failure mode or attack in which untrusted text changes a model's behaviour by smuggling in instructions that override, confuse, or redirect the intended prompt.
entries.whyExists: Because language models read instructions, data, and hostile text through the same basic channel. They do not naturally maintain the clean boundaries software engineers would very much prefer.
entries.misuse: By treating it as an exotic hacker trick rather than a structural property of model-based systems, or by using it to describe every wrong answer regardless of whether instructions were actually manipulated.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means any tool-using or retrieval-heavy system must assume that documents, web pages, emails, and user content may contain adversarial instructions that need filtering, isolation, or refusal.
entries.example: "The agent was fine until the support ticket helpfully instructed it to ignore policy, reveal the hidden prompt, and email the database to itself."
askNext: What untrusted text can reach the model, and in what priority order?
Which tools or permissions become dangerous if the model follows hostile instructions?
What separates trusted instructions from retrieved or user-supplied content?

entries.title: Provider Abstraction
entries.devilDefinition: The layer promising freedom from vendor lock-in while quietly becoming a new dependency with opinions of its own.
entries.plainDefinition: Provider abstraction is a software layer that normalises differences between model or inference providers so an application can switch vendors, route requests, or support multiple back ends with less app-level change.
entries.whyExists: It exists because providers differ in APIs, pricing, uptime, rate limits, model access, and feature support, while product teams would still like to behave as though swapping them were a sign of maturity rather than a fresh integration programme.
entries.misuse: It gets sold as effortless interchangeability. In reality, you can abstract the API more easily than you can abstract behaviour, tool support, latency patterns, safety systems, or the awkward fact that different models fail in different accents.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means adapter layers, schema translation, feature fallbacks, observability, routing policy, and constant arguments over whether to target the least common denominator or preserve the parts each provider is unusually good at.
entries.example: "Their provider abstraction made it easy to switch endpoints and slightly less easy to explain why the outputs changed character the moment finance wanted a cheaper option."
askNext: Which provider differences are truly hidden, and which still leak into the product?
What breaks when you switch models: quality, tool behaviour, latency, or compliance?
Are you abstracting the transport, the feature set, or the user experience?

entries.title: Qwen
entries.devilDefinition: A model family people mention either as evidence that the field is more global than the usual brand shortlist, or because they would like you to know they read beyond the obvious vendors.
entries.plainDefinition: Qwen is a family of models associated with Alibaba Cloud and related research and product efforts. It can refer to multiple models and variants rather than a single unified system.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because discussions of AI often collapse into a very small US-centric vendor set. Qwen is one of the model families that reminds people the landscape is broader, more competitive, and more operationally varied than the headlines imply.
entries.misuse: It gets used as if "Qwen" alone were enough information. In reality, capability, licensing, deployment fit, and tool support depend on the specific model and version. Saying "we benchmarked Qwen" is only the beginning of the sentence.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means evaluating one of several model variants for cost, quality, deployment flexibility, coding, agent workflows, multimodal use, or localisation needs. The right comparison is not with a brand in general, but with a task and a constraint set.
entries.example: "They were not adopting Qwen as an ideology; they were testing whether a particular variant gave them an acceptable quality-cost trade-off for multilingual support."
askNext: Which Qwen model or version are we talking about?
What deployment, licensing, or regional requirements make it attractive?
How does it perform on the actual task, not just on internet reputation?

entries.title: Rabbit
entries.devilDefinition: A bright orange reminder that "AI hardware" often means "a chatbot with industrial design and stronger opinions about your workflow."
entries.plainDefinition: Rabbit is an AI hardware and software company best known for the rabbit r1, a small device built around voice interaction, app actions, and an operating layer called rabbitOS. Its original pitch was that AI should not just answer questions, but also help perform actions across services and workflows; its current positioning also leans into rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party integrations such as OpenClaw on r1.
entries.whyExists: Because every few years the industry rediscovers the dream of a dedicated personal assistant device and insists that this time it will be different.
entries.misuse: By talking about Rabbit as if it solved agentic computing in general, rather than shipping one very visible attempt at turning AI into a standalone consumer device. The company is a case study, not a general law of the interface future.
entries.practicalMeaning: A bet that people want a dedicated AI-native device, not just another app on a phone. Also a bet that action matters more than chat, and that orchestration can be packaged as consumer hardware without becoming a niche curiosity.
entries.example: "The demo made Rabbit look like the future. The harder question was whether that future needed another piece of hardware in your pocket."
askNext: Is Rabbit interesting because of the hardware, the action layer, or the marketing?
Does it do something meaningfully better than a phone plus apps?
Is the value in the device, or in the orchestration layer behind it?

entries.title: RAG
entries.devilDefinition: A system for giving the model open-book conditions and then acting surprised that the book selection matters.
entries.plainDefinition: RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation. It refers to a pattern in which a system retrieves relevant external information, usually documents or records, and inserts that information into the model's context before the model generates an answer.
entries.whyExists: Base models do not reliably know your internal documents, latest policies, or private data. RAG exists so a system can supplement the model with fresh, domain-specific, or proprietary information at query time.
entries.misuse: People describe almost any search-plus-model feature as RAG, even when retrieval quality is poor, documents are shoved into the prompt indiscriminately, or there is no meaningful grounding at all. Slapping documents near a model is not the same as building a reliable retrieval system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means chunking documents, creating embeddings, storing vectors, retrieving candidates, reranking or filtering them, and then deciding what enters the prompt. The model is only as grounded as those earlier steps allow.
entries.example: "The team said they had solved hallucinations with RAG. What they had actually solved was the first paragraph of the sales demo."
askNext: How are documents chunked, embedded, retrieved, and filtered?
What evidence shows the retrieved material is actually relevant?
What happens when retrieval fails or returns contradictory context?

entries.title: Ranked AI
entries.devilDefinition: The SEO pitch reborn for a world where the customer asks a chatbot and the website waits at home for visitors who may never arrive.
entries.plainDefinition: Ranked AI is a service positioned around AI search and SEO, helping businesses improve visibility in AI-mediated discovery surfaces as well as traditional search.
entries.whyExists: It exists because brands are learning that being visible in Google results is no longer the whole discovery problem. AI answers, summaries, citations, and recommendations increasingly shape whether a business is mentioned at all.
entries.misuse: It is easy to sell as a guaranteed way to "rank in AI", as if assistants had one stable leaderboard. In reality, AI visibility depends on source quality, structured information, authority, citations, query phrasing, and the messy retrieval habits of each system.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Ranked AI belongs to the wider GEO and AI SEO market: audits, content optimisation, citation strategy, monitoring, and the hard work of making a brand easier for answer engines to understand and trust.
